title: Drug-induced Liver Injury from Hormonal and Non-hormonal Therapies: Insights from a Large Case Series
journal: Journal of Clinical and Experimental Hepatology
summary: Endometriosis is a chronic disease defined by the abnormal growth of endometrial tissue outside the uterus, predominantly in the peritoneum and ovaries, which triggers a cascade of inflammatory responses. This ectopic endometrial tissue is strongly associated with three major clinical manifestations: chronic pain, infertility, and the underlying inflammation that characterizes the disease pathology. The inflammatory environment generated by mislocalized endometrial tissue contributes significantly to both the symptomatic burden and reproductive dysfunction experienced by patients. These associations underscore the multifactorial nature of endometriosis as a systemic condition with interconnected pathophysiological consequences affecting quality of life and reproductive potential. Understanding these relationships is critical for developing therapeutic strategies that target the inflammatory mechanisms driving pain and infertility in endometriosis patients.

title: Abstracts from the 2024 Annual Meeting of the Society of General Internal Medicine
summary: Endometriosis is a chronic inflammatory disease characterized by multiple debilitating manifestations affecting reproductive, gastrointestinal, and pain pathways. The condition is strongly associated with chronic pelvic pain, infertility, and gastrointestinal dysfunction, all driven by underlying inflammatory mechanisms. These interconnected clinical features represent the primary burden of endometriosis on patient quality of life and reproductive health. The inflammatory basis of the disease underpins the development of pain symptoms and contributes to both fertility complications and gastrointestinal complications. Understanding these associations is critical for comprehensive patient management addressing the multisystem nature of endometriosis rather than isolated symptom treatment.

title: Uncovering moderators of pain perception by women with endometriosis from Latin America and Spain: the roles of sociodemographics, racial self-identity, and pain catastrophizing
summary: This study examined pain perception moderators in approximately 1,400 women with endometriosis from Latin America and Spain, identifying pain catastrophizing as a robust psychological predictor of symptom severity. While endometriosis causes multiple pain manifestations including dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and chronic pelvic pain, self-identified race and ethnicity did not significantly predict pain severity, suggesting that psychological factors transcend racial boundaries in this population. Pain catastrophizing scores were highly associated with severe pelvic pain across all pain types and demonstrated stronger predictive value than demographic variables, though older age was notably associated with increased dyspareunia severity. Higher educational attainment, particularly Master's degree completion, correlated with reduced severe pelvic pain reports, indicating a protective sociodemographic factor. Elevated pain catastrophizing scores were linked to poorer health-related quality of life, pain persistence, and reduced pharmacological treatment efficacy. These findings support incorporating psychological interventions such as Cognitive Behavioral Therapy and Acceptance and Commitment Therapy into endometriosis management to address pain catastrophizing and improve clinical outcomes.

title: Managing the menopause in general practice: a tale of pragmatism, caution, and optimism
journal: The British Journal of General Practice
summary: Endometriosis is a chronic inflammatory disease of the reproductive organs characterized by two major clinical manifestations: chronic pelvic pain and infertility. The condition's inflammatory nature drives both the pain symptoms experienced by patients and the reproductive dysfunction that frequently accompanies the disease. These interconnected effects—pain and fertility impairment—represent the primary clinical burden of endometriosis and are key considerations in managing affected women across the lifespan. The association between endometriosis and these dual complications highlights the need for comprehensive clinical approaches that address both symptom management and reproductive outcomes in general practice settings.

title: Intersection of menstrual and menopausal health with mental health: implications for general practice
journal: The British Journal of General Practice
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is a prevalent gynecological condition affecting women of reproductive age that presents with a constellation of debilitating symptoms including chronic pain, heavy menstrual bleeding, and infertility. The condition involves the abnormal presence of endometrial tissue outside its normal anatomical location, creating significant physical and reproductive morbidity. Beyond its somatic manifestations, endometriosis carries substantial mental health implications, suggesting an important intersection between reproductive and psychiatric health that warrants attention in general practice. The association between endometriosis and multiple reproductive complications—particularly infertility and menstrual dysfunction—compounds the psychological burden on affected women. General practitioners should recognize endometriosis not only as a gynecological condition but as a complex disorder with substantial impacts on mental wellbeing and quality of life. This integrated perspective is essential for comprehensive management and support of women experiencing endometriosis.

title: Evaluating the Current Evidence for the Efficacy of Cannabis in Symptom Management of Endometriosis-Associated Pain
summary: Endometriosis causes multiple debilitating symptoms including chronic pelvic pain, dyspareunia, dyschezia, and dysuria, substantially reducing quality of life in affected women. The underlying pathophysiology involves inflammation and dysregulation of the endocannabinoid system, with cannabinoid receptors CB1 and CB2 abnormally expressed in endometrial glands of women with endometriosis but absent in normal ovarian tissue. Cannabis and its primary cannabinoids THC and CBD demonstrate therapeutic potential through multiple mechanisms: reducing inflammatory mediators, inhibiting endometrial tissue growth, providing analgesic effects, and modulating pain perception through the endocannabinoid system. Self-reported survey data show cannabis efficacy in reducing pelvic pain, improving sleep, and alleviating anxiety and depression—comorbidities common in endometriosis patients—while also decreasing reliance on conventional analgesics. CB1 receptor distribution in pain-processing brain regions (amygdala, hippocampus, cerebral cortex) and anxiety circuits further supports cannabinoids' potential for managing both nociceptive and psychological symptoms. Despite promising preclinical evidence and consistent patient-reported benefits, the authors note a critical gap: lack of randomized controlled trials to definitively establish cannabis efficacy for endometriosis-related pain management.

title: History of infertility and risk of endometrial cancer in the Women’s Health Initiative
journal: Cancer epidemiology, biomarkers & prevention : a publication of the American Association for Cancer Research, cosponsored by the American Society of Preventive Oncology
summary:
# Summary

This epidemiological study examined the relationship between infertility history and endometrial cancer risk in postmenopausal women from the Women's Health Initiative cohort. While overall infertility showed no statistically significant association with incident endometrial cancer, a notable association emerged specifically for prevalent cases, with the strongest effect observed for infertility due to endometriosis (odds ratio 2.42). The increased cancer risk appears partially mediated by reproductive and metabolic factors, as infertile women demonstrated higher smoking rates, lower body mass index despite associations with polycystic ovary syndrome, and earlier menopause compared to parous controls. These findings suggest that the infertility-endometrial cancer relationship is complex and may be driven by specific etiologic causes of infertility, particularly endometriosis-related infertility, rather than infertility as a uniform clinical entity.

title: Validity and Reliability of the Hungarian Version of the Pain Self-Efficacy Questionnaire Among Women with Endometriosis and Chronic Pelvic Pain
summary: Endometriosis is strongly associated with chronic pelvic pain and significantly reduces health-related quality of life by contributing to fatigue, anxiety, and depression. Pain-related self-efficacy—a person's confidence in managing pain—plays a critical psychological role in coping with chronic pain and is inversely associated with pain intensity, pain catastrophizing, and perceived stress. The Hungarian version of the Pain Self-Efficacy Questionnaire (PSEQ-HU) was validated in women with endometriosis and chronic pelvic pain, demonstrating strong correlations with multiple health domains including bodily pain, physical functioning, vitality, and social functioning. Higher pain-related self-efficacy was consistently associated with better overall health-related quality of life, lower pain catastrophizing, and improved physical and mental health outcomes. The PSEQ-HU proved to be a reliable and valid assessment tool for measuring pain-related self-efficacy in this population, suggesting its utility in evaluating treatment effectiveness and guiding psychological interventions for endometriosis-related chronic pain management.

title: Observational and Genetic Analyses of Traumatic Experiences and Endometriosis
summary: This case-control and genetic study reveals a robust bidirectional association between traumatic experiences and endometriosis, with women affected by endometriosis reporting significantly higher rates of childhood and adulthood trauma, including emotional, physical, and sexual abuse. Genetic correlation analyses identified shared genetic architectures linking endometriosis to posttraumatic stress disorder and childhood maltreatment, suggesting common biological pathways underlying these conditions. The research demonstrates that both traumatic experiences and genetic predisposition function as independent risk factors for endometriosis development, with an endometriosis polygenic risk score strongly associated with disease susceptibility. Contact trauma emerged as a particularly notable risk factor in the association with endometriosis. These findings suggest that trauma-related psychological and genetic factors may play meaningful roles in endometriosis pathogenesis, potentially informing integrated approaches to patient assessment and management that address both the gynecological and mental health dimensions of the disease.

title: The Role of GnRH Agonist Therapy and Hysterosalpingography (HSG) in Diagnosing and Treating Tubal Factor Infertility
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis, a chronic multisystem disease affecting approximately 10% of women of reproductive age, causes infertility in about one-third of affected women through multiple mechanisms including chronic inflammation, hormonal dependence, adhesions, and cyst development, while also triggering pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and dysuria through elevated prostaglandins and leukotrienes. GnRH agonists such as triptorelin acetate effectively treat endometriosis by suppressing ovarian activity and reducing inflammation; in one clinical case, triptorelin acetate downregulated pituitary function, decreased a cervical cystic lesion from 19×12.5 mm to 9×6 mm, and reduced elevated CA125 levels within 14 days. For patients with endometriosis-related infertility complicated by bilateral hydrosalpinx, which dramatically reduces natural conception rates, in vitro fertilization combined with controlled ovarian hyperstimulation and frozen embryo transfer offers improved outcomes, with additional GnRH agonist and hormone replacement therapy optimizing endometrial preparation for embryo implantation. Hysterosalpingography serves as a useful diagnostic tool for detecting tubal pathology, while endometriosis patients undergoing assisted reproduction face increased risks of postoperative complications including placenta previa. This case demonstrates how integrated medical management with hormonal therapy followed by reproductive technology can overcome the multifactorial fertility barriers imposed by endometriosis.

title: Recent Advances and Clinical Outcomes of Endometriosis
summary: Endometriosis is a chronic inflammatory disease affecting approximately 10% of reproductive-age women with significant clinical consequences. The condition is strongly associated with infertility, chronic pain, and substantially reduced quality of life, making it a major healthcare burden for affected patients. These multifaceted manifestations underscore the need for comprehensive management strategies that address both the reproductive and systemic impacts of the disease. The combination of pain symptoms and fertility challenges creates complex clinical presentations that require integrated treatment approaches spanning gynecological, medical, and potentially psychological interventions.

title: Cine-MRI for Quantifying Uterine Peristalsis: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis
summary:
# Summary

This systematic review and meta-analysis examines uterine peristalsis—the wave-like contractions of the uterus controlled by estrogen and progesterone—as measured by cine-MRI imaging. Normal cervix-to-fundus contractions are essential for sperm transport and embryo implantation, but endometriosis, adenomyosis, fibroids, and PCOS all significantly impair these contractile patterns. The meta-analysis reveals that women with endometriosis show markedly increased uterine contractility during the luteal phase compared to healthy controls, along with abnormal contraction frequencies and directions that may facilitate retrograde menstruation and reduce implantation rates. These findings suggest that altered uterine peristalsis is a mechanical contributor to the infertility and reduced implantation associated with endometriosis. Cine-MRI has proven valuable as a diagnostic tool for identifying these contractility abnormalities in reproductive pathologies, offering potential to explain how structural and hormonal factors in endometriosis compromise fertility.

title: High prevalence of undiagnosed iron deficiency in endometriosis patients: A cross‐sectional study
journal: International Journal of Gynaecology and Obstetrics
summary: This cross-sectional study reveals a high prevalence of undiagnosed iron deficiency (ID) in endometriosis patients, with iron deficiency anemia occurring in 13.5% of cases and ID detected in 35.7% of patients using transferrin saturation (TSAT) testing. Iron deficiency is strongly associated with endometriosis through multiple pathways: heavy menstrual bleeding common in endometriosis patients (especially those with concurrent adenomyosis) drives iron loss, while the chronic inflammatory state characteristic of endometriosis dysregulates the hepcidin-ferroportin pathway, impairing intestinal iron absorption. Iron deficiency manifests with multiple debilitating symptoms—including fatigue, weakness, dizziness, sleep disturbances, and depression—that substantially reduce quality of life in endometriosis patients. The study demonstrates that transferrin saturation is a more specific diagnostic measure of ID than ferritin alone, particularly important since ferritin is an acute-phase reactant that may be artificially elevated in the inflammatory milieu of endometriosis. Comorbid conditions such as inflammatory bowel disease further compound iron absorption impairment in these patients. These findings suggest that iron status screening and supplementation should be integrated into endometriosis management to address both the underlying anemia and its contribution to fatigue and reduced quality of life.

title: Pelvic pain & endometriosis: the development of a patient-centred e-health resource for those affected by endometriosis-associated dyspareunia
journal: BMC Medical Informatics and Decision Making
summary: Endometriosis causes a constellation of symptoms including dyspareunia (painful intercourse), dysmenorrhea, chronic pelvic pain, dyschezia, dysuria, and fatigue, with dyspareunia affecting approximately half of affected individuals. Endometriosis-associated dyspareunia significantly compromises sexual function by reducing sexual arousal, desire, and satisfaction. Beyond its physical manifestations, dyspareunia triggers substantial psychological consequences including anxiety, guilt, embarrassment, and reduced self-esteem, which can lead to sexual avoidance and relationship strain. The article describes development of a patient-centered e-health resource designed to address these interconnected physical and psychological impacts of endometriosis-associated dyspareunia. This digital intervention acknowledges the multidimensional nature of the condition, recognizing that dyspareunia's effects extend beyond pain to encompass emotional well-being and sexual relationships. The e-health approach represents an effort to provide accessible, stigma-reducing support for individuals navigating the complex burden of endometriosis-related pelvic pain.

title: Causal Links Between Brain Functional Networks and Endometriosis: A Large-Scale Genetic-Driven Observational Study
summary:
# Summary

This large-scale genetic-driven study establishes causal links between brain functional networks and endometriosis manifestations, revealing a complex neurobiological architecture underlying the disorder. Cerebellar activity, particularly within the Subcortical-Cerebellum network, appears protective against fallopian tube endometriosis and chronic pain, while enhanced functional connectivity in the Default Mode Network and other higher-order cognitive networks is associated with increased adenomyosis risk and ovarian endometriosis susceptibility. The central nervous system contributes to endometriosis onset and progression through multiple pathways: central sensitization amplifies chronic pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, and dyspareunia, while hyperactivity in the Default Mode and Central Executive Networks correlates with pain chronicity and emotional dysregulation. Additionally, endometriosis-related neuroinflammation and glial activation impair cognitive function, manifesting as brain fog and cognitive impairments that reflect CNS involvement beyond peripheral pain pathways. These findings demonstrate that psychological stress and emotional burden exacerbate endometriosis severity, establishing bidirectional brain-endometriosis interactions that implicate emotion regulation, sensory processing, and cognitive control as key mechanistic targets for therapeutic intervention.

title: Analyzing the association between menstrual coitus and endometriosis’ pathogenesis: A narrative review
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is characterized by ectopic endometrial tissue growth accompanied by pelvic adhesions and inflammatory responses, with pathogenesis involving multiple interrelated mechanisms. Menstrual coitus and orgasm during menstruation may increase endometriosis risk by promoting retrograde menstruation through enhanced uterine contractions and increased intrauterine pressure, though evidence is conflicting across studies. Disrupted immune homeostasis and the immune system's inability to clear ectopic endometrial cells contribute significantly to disease development, compounded by genital microbiota imbalance that modifies immune responses, estrogen metabolism, and endometrial cell phenotype. Sexual activity during menstruation creates a window of vulnerability by facilitating pathogen ascent through increased cervical mucus penetrability, reduced anti-infectious immunity, and disruption of the vaginal microbiome—potentially leading to chronic endometritis and low-grade pelvic inflammation that augments uterine contractility and endometriosis risk. The condition is strongly associated with dysmenorrhea and dyspareunia, and evidence indicates that genital tract infections and microbiota dysbiosis may be both consequences of and contributors to endometriosis pathogenesis.

title: Self-reported and tracker-estimated physical activity outcomes in women with chronic pelvic pain disorders: A longitudinal evaluation of construct validity
summary:
# Summary

Women with chronic pelvic pain disorders (CPPDs), predominantly endometriosis, experience significantly reduced physical activity levels and prolonged sedentary behavior, conditions associated with elevated risk for cardiovascular disease, metabolic dysfunction, depression, and mortality. Despite these risks, physical activity demonstrates important symptom-reducing effects in this population, though barriers including fatigue and fear of symptom exacerbation limit engagement. This longitudinal study evaluated the construct validity of the International Physical Activity Questionnaire (IPAQ) against Fitbit-measured activity in women with CPPDs, finding that IPAQ-reported walking minutes showed the strongest agreement with objective step counts and light-to-moderate activity measures, while total activity and sedentary time estimates demonstrated lower inter-method agreement. The results indicate that IPAQ walking items and total activity measures may be most suitable for tracking individual-level changes over time in this population, though factors such as fatigue, sleep quality, and opioid use can impair self-report accuracy. These findings provide practical guidance for selecting valid physical activity assessment tools in clinical and research settings for women with CPPDs, supporting both individual patient monitoring and population-level health outcome tracking.

title: Navigating the Limits of Diagnosis: Young Adults' Experiences of Chronic Living
summary:
# Summary

This qualitative study examines how young adults navigate the diagnosis and ongoing management of chronic illnesses including endometriosis, inflammatory bowel disease, chronic fatigue syndrome, and rheumatoid arthritis. While diagnosis provides crucial relief and legitimacy that enables medical treatment, it simultaneously creates an emotionally complex and contested space marked by ambivalence, as the promised clarity of diagnosis often fails to match the unstable reality of living with chronic symptoms. Chronic illness broadly generates significant uncertainty, identity loss, and disruption to normative life trajectories, with patients experiencing debilitating manifestations such as pain and fatigue that remain underacknowledged by healthcare systems and society. The study reveals a critical gap between diagnosis as a gatekeeping mechanism for medical care and its failure to adequately address the substantial emotional toll and psychological burden of symptom management. These findings underscore that while achieving diagnosis represents an important practical milestone, the diagnostic process itself—and the subsequent ongoing navigation of chronic illness—demands greater recognition of its emotional and identity-related consequences for young adults managing long-term conditions.

title: The meaning of repeated assisted reproductive technologies failures experienced of older infertile women
summary:
# Summary

This qualitative study examines the psychological and clinical experiences of older infertile women undergoing repeated assisted reproductive technology (ART) treatments. Advanced maternal age significantly reduces reproductive capacity, with natural pregnancy rates dropping to approximately 50% for women over 35 and IVF success rates declining to 25% with elevated miscarriage rates of 30-50% in this population. While ART treats infertility, repeated treatment failures accumulate into serious depressive disorders, and an infertility diagnosis itself triggers substantial psychological distress including depression, social isolation, inferiority complexes, and self-blame. Participants reported additional stressors from hormone therapy-induced emotional fluctuations, embryo implantation failures causing profound grief, and underlying conditions such as endometriosis that compound treatment difficulty. The study highlights that the combined burden of age-related reproductive decline, repeated procedural failures, hormonal interventions, and the emotional attachment to failed embryos creates a complex cascade of mental health challenges beyond the biological barriers to conception.

title: Association of urinary tract endometriosis with physical and mental health: a cross-sectional study
summary:
# Summary

This cross-sectional study examined how urinary tract endometriosis (UTE) impacts physical and mental health outcomes in affected patients. Endometriosis, particularly the deep infiltrating (DIE) and urinary tract forms, causes multiple pain syndromes including dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and chronic pelvic pain, while UTE specifically produces urinary symptoms such as dysuria, urgency, and painful micturition. Untreated UTE leads to serious urologic complications including hydronephrosis and hydroureter, with delayed diagnosis potentially resulting in irreversible kidney damage. Patients with UTE demonstrated significantly elevated urinary biomarkers (creatinine, leukocytes, erythrocytes, and protein) and higher rates of urologic abnormalities requiring surgical intervention such as ureteral stent implantation. The study found that chronic pain and painkiller dependency substantially reduced physical health scores in UTE patients, while reproductive complications (multiple miscarriages) and awareness of urologic abnormalities before surgery significantly lowered mental health scores, with additional psychological burden arising from fear of kidney damage and surgical anxiety. These findings highlight that UTE presents a multisystem disease with substantial impacts on both physical and mental health quality of life.

title: Pelvic peritoneal endometriosis is linked to the endometrial inflammatory profile: a prospective cohort study
summary:
# Summary

Pelvic peritoneal endometriosis is an estrogen-driven inflammatory condition that creates a pro-inflammatory peritoneal microenvironment and impairs endometrial receptivity, thereby reducing fertility. Peritoneal endometriosis specifically increases the risk of an inflammatory immunohistochemical endometrial profile by over threefold, whereas Stage III endometriosis reduces this inflammatory risk by 80%, suggesting complex stage-dependent inflammatory patterns. Chronic endometritis, which occurs at significantly higher prevalence in women with endometriosis (29.41% compared to controls), further compromises endometrial receptivity and is associated with abnormal uterine bleeding and infertility. The inflammatory imbalance extends beyond the peritoneal cavity to the uterine cavity, impairing embryo implantation through multiple mechanisms including local immune dysfunction, hormonal imbalances, diminished ovarian reserve, and disrupted anatomical relationships of pelvic organs. These findings suggest that peritoneal endometriosis-related inflammation may be mechanistically linked to chronic endometritis, establishing a potential two-site inflammatory pathology that contributes to reduced fertility in endometriosis patients.

title: Endometriosis and risk of depression among oral contraceptive users: a pooled analysis of cohort studies from 13 countries
summary: This pooled analysis of 13 cohort studies demonstrates that women with endometriosis have a significantly elevated risk of depression, with a hazard ratio of 1.85, even among oral contraceptive users. The primary mechanism linking endometriosis to depression appears to be chronic pelvic pain, which is experienced by approximately 75% of women with endometriosis and mediates much of the observed mental health effect. However, the study reveals that depression risk remains elevated in women with endometriosis even without pain symptoms, suggesting that non-pain factors such as infertility, gastrointestinal complications, fatigue, and shared genetic predispositions also contribute to mental health burden. Women with endometriosis show substantially higher rates of hospitalization (18.6% versus 7.4%) and surgical interventions, indicating greater disease severity and healthcare utilization. While oral contraceptives effectively reduce endometriosis-related pain, they do not fully eliminate the elevated depression risk, indicating that comprehensive mental health management should be integrated into endometriosis care alongside pain management strategies.

title: Association between endometriosis and working life among Danish women
summary: This Danish study demonstrates that endometriosis has substantial impacts on women's working life and economic productivity. Women with endometriosis experience multiple debilitating symptoms—including chronic pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, fatigue, and infertility—that collectively impair their ability to work. The condition is associated with significantly higher rates of sick leave, disability pension claims, and early labour market exit, with affected women losing approximately 0.26 additional working years compared to the general female population. Women with endometriosis report lower work ability scores, reduced workforce participation, increased absenteeism, and greater work disturbances, with productivity loss correlating with symptom severity. Physical pain directly interferes with both paid employment and household responsibilities, contributing to widespread work inefficiency. The study also identifies diagnostic delay as an additional factor associated with reduced work ability, suggesting that earlier diagnosis and intervention could help mitigate occupational disability in this population.

title: Women’s Health Study (WENDY)—a protocol of a population-based study assessing gynecological and metabolic health in women in their mid-30s
summary: The Women's Health Study (WENDY) is a population-based investigation examining gynecological and metabolic health in women in their mid-30s, a critical age window when major gynecological diseases including endometriosis and polycystic ovary syndrome typically manifest. Multiple gynecological conditions—including endometriosis, heavy menstrual bleeding, vulvodynia, and polycystic ovary syndrome—are strongly associated with reduced quality of life in fertile-aged women. The study recognizes that metabolic derangements are increasingly prevalent in women and significantly impact both gynecological health and overall quality of life, suggesting bidirectional relationships between metabolic and reproductive health. WENDY aims to comprehensively investigate the prevalence of gynecological diseases and symptoms while assessing their broader effects on overall health and well-being. By focusing on women in their mid-30s, the study captures a population in which gynecological disorders have already manifested, enabling investigation of the long-term consequences of these conditions on metabolic status and quality of life. This integrated approach positions WENDY to elucidate the interconnected pathways linking gynecological disease, metabolic dysfunction, and women's health outcomes.

title: Consensus on Symptom Selection for Endometriosis Questionnaires: A Modified e‐Delphi Study
summary: This modified e-Delphi study established a consensus-based symptom framework for endometriosis assessment questionnaires by surveying both patients and healthcare professionals. Six key symptoms and consequences achieved strong consensus (>70% agreement), including menstrual pain, pain during sexual intercourse, cyclic pain during defecation, infertility, cyclic pelvic pain, and increased healthcare visits due to abdominal or pelvic pain. The study identified a broader symptom spectrum associated with endometriosis, encompassing gastrointestinal symptoms, fatigue, heavy menstrual bleeding, and work/school absences, though consensus varied between patient and clinician perspectives. Notably, patients emphasized emotional and psychosocial impacts more than healthcare providers, who prioritized prognostic indicators such as pain severity and functional impairment. The symptom clustering approach is proposed as important for predicting endometriosis and improving diagnostic timelines, addressing the significant diagnostic delays attributed to lack of awareness and missed diagnoses.

title: Development of the COMPASS model of endometriosis: A COmprehensive model of pain encompassing agency, systemic factors and sense making
summary:
# Summary

This study presents the COMPASS model, a comprehensive biopsychosocial framework explaining endometriosis-related pain that extends beyond biological mechanisms to encompass psychological, social, and systemic factors. Endometriosis causes multifaceted symptoms including debilitating pain, fatigue, sleep disruptions, gastrointestinal disruptions, and infertility, with endometrial-like cell infiltration triggering chronic inflammation. The research reveals that endometriosis-related pain substantially worsens quality of life and mental health, while depression, anxiety, and pain catastrophizing further exacerbate pain severity. Critically, the COMPASS model demonstrates that patients' pain experiences are shaped by individual agency and sense-making processes, as well as systemic barriers including delayed diagnosis, healthcare invalidation, distrust in medical professionals, limited treatment access, and inadequate financial support. Invalidating experiences and lack of provider education about endometriosis particularly intensify psychological distress and pain levels, whereas validated healthcare interactions reduce symptoms. The model emphasizes that sustainable management requires not only medical interventions—such as surgical excision and hormonal therapies—but also psychological support and systemic healthcare improvements that address the gendered nature of endometriosis recognition and ensure patient-centered validation throughout care.

title: Clinical features of patients with endometriosis on the cesarean scar
journal: The Kaohsiung Journal of Medical Sciences
summary: Endometriosis, a chronic inflammatory disease affecting approximately 10% of women of reproductive age, is strongly associated with both infertility and pelvic pain. The pathogenesis of endometriosis involves endometrial dysfunction, which represents a key pathological feature of the disease. Endometrial stem cells play a critical role in maintaining normal endometrial regeneration and tissue homeostasis, and stem cell exhaustion may contribute to the endometrial dysfunction observed in endometriosis patients. This study on cesarean scar endometriosis highlights how disruption of stem cell function and endometrial integrity can lead to ectopic endometrial growth in vulnerable anatomical locations. Understanding the relationship between stem cell dysfunction and endometrial pathology provides insights into the mechanisms underlying endometriosis development and may inform future therapeutic strategies targeting these cellular processes.

title: Robotic reconstruction for benign upper urinary tract obstruction: a review of the current literature
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis represents a significant benign etiology of upper urinary tract obstruction, contributing to ureteral stricture formation through disease infiltration and fibrotic mechanisms. The literature indicates that endometriosis accounts for a notable proportion of benign ureteral strictures, alongside other causes such as stones, infection, and retroperitoneal fibrosis. This obstruction can impair renal function and contribute to patient morbidity if left untreated. Robotic reconstruction techniques offer minimally invasive surgical approaches for managing these benign obstructions caused by endometriosis and other etiologies. The review emphasizes the importance of recognizing endometriosis as a potential cause of upper urinary tract obstruction and discusses contemporary surgical management strategies to preserve renal function and improve patient outcomes.

title: The impact of endometriosis on patients’ quality of sexual life
summary: Endometriosis significantly impairs sexual quality of life through multiple interconnected mechanisms. The condition causes chronic pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, and dyspareunia, which collectively contribute to widespread sexual dysfunction including low sexual desire, impaired arousal, lubrication difficulties, and orgasmic disorders. The anticipation and fear of pain from dyspareunia inhibits the normal sexual response cycle, leading to sexual avoidance and diminished sexual pleasure, while also generating psychological distress including depression, anxiety, guilt toward partners, and reduced self-esteem. Additionally, the associated infertility further compounds sexual dysfunction and psychological burden. Notably, surgical excision of endometriotic lesions provides substantial therapeutic benefit, with 81.4% of patients reporting improvements in sexual quality of life and significant reductions in dyspareunia pain intensity, along with improvements in sexual desire and arousal within 6-12 months post-operatively.

title: Hospitalisations and length of stays in women with endometriosis: a data linkage prospective cohort study
summary: This prospective cohort study demonstrates that endometriosis imposes a substantial burden on healthcare utilization in Australian women, affecting approximately 14% of the population by mid-age. Women with endometriosis experience significantly higher hospital admission rates compared to unaffected women; notably, pelvic peritoneal endometriosis is the most common form identified in hospitalized patients, followed by uterine and ovarian endometriosis. The condition manifests through multiple debilitating symptoms including dysmenorrhea, heavy menstrual bleeding, fatigue, bowel problems, and associated mental health effects such as depression and anxiety. While surgical intervention effectively treats pain and infertility, postoperative recurrence is common, perpetuating a cycle of repeated surgical interventions and increased healthcare costs. Interestingly, although women with endometriosis have more frequent hospital admissions, they paradoxically have shorter individual stays and higher same-day discharge rates. The persistent elevation in hospitalizations and hospital days following diagnosis, compared to pre-diagnosis baseline, underscores the chronic and recurrent nature of endometriosis and its significant impact on quality of life and healthcare resources.

title: The Impact of Oxidized Regenerated Cellulose Application during Cystectomy and Drainage in Endometrioma Patients: A Retrospective Cohort Study
summary: Endometriomas, which affect 17–44% of endometriosis patients, are strongly associated with infertility and contribute to the pain and psychological distress experienced by approximately 70% and 50% of endometriosis patients respectively. This study compared two surgical approaches for treating endometriomas: cystectomy, which effectively suppresses recurrence but significantly reduces ovarian reserve and anti-Mullerian hormone (AMH) levels, and drainage with oxidized regenerated cellulose (ORC) application, which preserves ovarian reserve while maintaining comparable recurrence rates. Both surgical approaches successfully reduced pain through destruction of endometriotic lesions, though the benefit was primarily observed in dysmenorrhea reduction. The ORC application appears to work by creating an acidic microenvironment that triggers vasoconstriction and tissue anoxia, thereby reducing recurrence risk. While cystectomy with ORC produced greater AMH reduction than drainage with ORC (3.0±1.8 to 2.1±1.5 versus 1.4±1.25 to 1.1±0.9), there was no significant difference in recurrence rates between groups, suggesting that drainage with ORC may offer a more fertility-preserving alternative for endometrioma management.

title: Sleep Health and Psychological Wellbeing in Adult Women: A Specific Focus on Endometriosis—A Survey Study
summary: Endometriosis, characterized by the displacement of endometrial tissue to ectopic sites such as the ovaries and peritoneum, causes chronic pain and infertility while significantly impacting psychological health in affected women. The disease creates a bidirectional relationship between pain and sleep disturbance, wherein chronic endometriosis-related pain directly impairs sleep quality, while sleep deprivation conversely increases inflammation and pain perception, establishing a vicious cycle. Women with endometriosis exhibit markedly higher prevalence rates of severe insomnia (7.1%), anxiety symptoms (17.8%), and depressive symptoms (7.7%) compared to healthy controls, with sleep health showing significant associations with both disease stage and psychological difficulties. Hormonal dysregulation in endometriosis—as an estrogen-dependent pathology—disrupts the sleep-wake cycle through fluctuations in estradiol and progesterone, while sleep disturbances independently amplify emotional dysregulation, leading to reduced motivation and impaired emotion management. Sleep impairment directly contributes to psychological distress through multiple pathways, establishing a strong bidirectional relationship between sleep health and overall psychological wellbeing in women with endometriosis. These findings highlight the critical interconnection between endometriosis-related pain, sleep dysfunction, and mental health deterioration, suggesting that comprehensive management addressing all three domains may be essential for improving outcomes in this patient population.

title: Müllerian anomalies and endometriosis as potential explanatory models for the retrograde menstruation/implantation and the embryonic remnants/celomic metaplasia pathogenic theories: a systematic review and meta-analysis
summary:
# Summary

This systematic review and meta-analysis examines whether Müllerian anomalies support pathogenic theories of endometriosis development. The analysis demonstrates that obstructive Müllerian anomalies (OMA) are strongly associated with endometriosis, with a 47% prevalence compared to 19% in those without anomalies, supporting the retrograde menstruation/transtubal reflux theory where menstrual blood flows backward through the fallopian tubes and implants ectopically. Specific OMA subtypes including cervical atresia, hematocolpos, hematometra, and hemivaginal obstruction show particularly high endometriosis prevalence, with the delay from symptom onset to surgical correction emerging as a significant risk factor. Nonobstructive Müllerian anomalies (NOMA) show similar or only slightly elevated endometriosis prevalence compared to women without anomalies, suggesting that while retrograde menstruation appears the primary mechanism, secondary factors including myometrial dysfunction, uterine dysperistalsis, genetic modifications, and epigenetic changes may contribute to lesion development. The findings confirm endometriosis strongly associates with infertility and pelvic pain, supporting retrograde menstruation as the predominant pathogenic mechanism while not excluding alternative embryonic remnant or celomic metaplasia pathways in selected cases.

title: The health-promoting lifestyle and its relationship with the impacts of endometriosis on women’s lives in Iran, 2022: a cross-sectional study
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is characterized by ectopic implantation of endometrial tissue in the pelvis—most commonly affecting the ovaries, uterosacral ligaments, sigmoid colon, and peritoneum—triggering chronic inflammatory reactions that produce multiple debilitating symptoms including dysmenorrhea, chronic pelvic pain, dyspareunia, bloating, and infertility, while also imposing significant psychological burdens including anxiety and depression. Beyond physical manifestations, endometriosis substantially impacts women's quality of life across occupational, educational, social, and sexual domains, with the disease showing particular effects on fertility and reproductive function. A health-promoting lifestyle, particularly interventions emphasizing spiritual growth, stress control, nutritional improvements (increased fruits and vegetables, reduced red meat and saturated fat intake), and enhanced interpersonal relationships, demonstrates significant capacity to mitigate both physical and psychological impacts of endometriosis. Formal health promotion and education models have shown efficacy in improving overall lifestyle and reducing pain in affected women. The study identifies retrograde menstruation as a widely accepted causative factor and reveals comorbidities including hypothyroidism, rheumatoid arthritis, and allergies in a subset of endometriosis patients, suggesting broader systemic involvement.

title: Burden of somatic morbidity associated with a surgically verified diagnosis of endometriosis at a young age: a register-based follow-up cohort study in Finland
summary: This Finnish register-based cohort study demonstrates that women with surgically verified endometriosis diagnosed at a young age experience substantial somatic morbidity across multiple organ systems. Women with endometriosis had dramatically elevated rates of infertility (38% versus 9% by age 40), genital tract infections, pain conditions including dysmenorrhea and dyspareunia, and chronic pain syndromes, with heightened pain sensitivity manifesting as regional hyperalgesia and allodynia. Beyond reproductive and pain-related complications, endometriosis was significantly associated with autoimmune diseases, thyroid disorders, asthma, cardiovascular conditions, and allergic manifestations. Notably, women with deep endometriosis reported more severe pain compared to those with isolated ovarian endometriomas, and pre-existing depression or anxiety substantially increased the risk of additional somatic comorbidities in endometriosis patients. These findings underscore endometriosis as a systemic inflammatory condition extending far beyond the reproductive tract, with mental health status further amplifying overall disease burden.

title: The effect of educational intervention based on protection motivation theory in improving reproductive health protective behaviors: a quasi-experimental study
summary:
# Summary

This quasi-experimental study evaluated the effectiveness of an educational intervention based on Protection Motivation Theory (PMT) in promoting reproductive health protective behaviors among women. The research identified multiple factors associated with infertility, including endometriosis, polycystic ovary syndrome, infectious diseases (E. coli, gonorrhea, tuberculosis), metabolic conditions (diabetes, hypertension, hypothyroidism), and lifestyle factors such as smoking, obesity, stress, poor diet, and delayed childbearing. The PMT-based intervention significantly improved all key psychological constructs in the intervention group compared to controls, including perceived susceptibility, perceived severity, self-efficacy, protection motivation, and fear—ultimately leading to improved reproductive health protective behaviors. Notably, the study demonstrated that addressing modifiable risk factors through targeted education, such as reducing caffeine and fast food consumption, maintaining healthy weight, regular physical exercise, and practicing proper hygiene, can effectively reduce infertility risk and enhance reproductive health outcomes. These findings support PMT as a robust theoretical framework for designing health promotion interventions that translate protective cognitions into sustained behavioral change regarding reproductive health.

title: Psychological Distress and Quality of Life in Women With Endometriosis: A Narrative Review of Therapeutic Approaches and Challenges
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis causes a multifaceted burden on women through chronic pelvic pain, fatigue, and infertility, compounded by significant psychological distress including anxiety, depression, and social isolation. The underlying pathophysiology involves ectopic endometrial-like tissue growth outside the uterus, triggering systemic inflammation through immune-mediated cytokine release that directly drives pain, dysmenorrhea, and fertility problems, while simultaneously dysregulating the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis and elevating inflammatory cytokines that contribute to mood disorders. The psychological impact is further amplified by persistent pain and fertility issues, which together with delayed medical diagnosis—perpetuated by social stigma surrounding menstrual health—create bidirectional relationships between physical and mental health symptoms that severely impair daily functioning and work capacity. Treatment approaches are multimodal, including pharmacological options such as NSAIDs, oral contraceptives, GnRH agonists, and surgical laparoscopy for symptom relief, alongside psychological interventions like cognitive behavioral therapy and mindfulness-based stress reduction. Complementary strategies including moderate exercise, yoga, acupuncture, and anti-inflammatory dietary interventions with Mediterranean diet principles, omega-3 fatty acids, and herbal supplements like turmeric and ginger show promise in reducing both inflammatory processes and pain while improving overall quality of life. Support groups and social-psychological interventions are essential for addressing the social isolation and psychological consequences inherent to the disease.

title: Laparoscopically confirmed endometriosis and the risk of incident NAFLD: a prospective cohort study
summary: This prospective cohort study demonstrates that laparoscopically confirmed endometriosis significantly increases the risk of nonalcoholic fatty liver disease (NAFLD), with a 17% elevated hazard ratio. The association is partially mediated by surgical interventions (hysterectomy/oophorectomy accounting for 31.6% of the effect) and metabolic factors including hypercholesterolemia, hypertension, and infertility. The underlying mechanisms appear to involve shared pathophysiological pathways: endometriosis generates chronic systemic proinflammatory states characterized by elevated tumor necrosis factor-α, interleukin-1, and interleukin-6, all of which promote NAFLD development, while oxidative stress also contributes to hepatic lipid accumulation in both conditions. The association is particularly pronounced in women younger than 50 years, suggesting age-dependent susceptibility. These findings broaden the recognized comorbidity profile of endometriosis beyond reproductive dysfunction and pelvic pain to include significant extrapelvic metabolic and hepatic disease, with implications for long-term cardiovascular risk management in affected women.

title: Analysis of the Biopsychosocial Impacts Associated with Endometriosis to Improve Patient Care
summary: Endometriosis is a complex inflammatory condition caused by ectopic endometrial tissue growth outside the uterus, arising through multiple mechanisms including retrograde menstruation, coelomic metaplasia, and immune system dysfunction. The disease manifests through diverse physical symptoms—chronic pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, menorrhagia, and gastrointestinal disturbances—alongside substantial psychological burden including depression, anxiety, and sleep disruption. Pathophysiologically, endometriosis involves dysregulated estrogen signaling through altered aromatase and estrogen receptor expression, progesterone resistance, elevated inflammatory cytokines (IL-1, IL-6, TNF-α), macrophage activation, and impaired natural killer cell function, with genetic susceptibility linked to WNT4, VEZT, and CDKN2B-AS1 polymorphisms. Beyond reproductive complications including infertility, the condition significantly impairs quality of life through work absenteeism, relationship strain, and social isolation, with hormonal treatments introducing additional burdens such as weight gain and diminished bone density. Both surgical intervention via laparoscopy and medical management offer symptom relief, though comprehensive biopsychosocial care addressing physical pain, mental health, and lifestyle factors is essential for optimizing patient outcomes.

title: Endometriosis Communities on Reddit: Quantitative Analysis
summary: Endometriosis, characterized by the presence of uterine lining tissue outside the uterus, causes multiple debilitating symptoms including chronic pelvic pain, painful menstruation, dyspareunia, dysuria, constipation, and infertility, collectively resulting in severe impairment of quality of life. Clinical management involves hormonal therapy, surgical removal of lesions, laparoscopic excision for diagnosis and treatment, and fertility interventions. This analysis of endometriosis communities on Reddit demonstrates that online health communities provide substantial therapeutic benefits beyond traditional medical care, including emotional support, validation, improved disease knowledge, and symptom management strategies that help patients feel reassured and empowered. Notably, engagement in these online support groups correlates with positive emotional expression and recovery outcomes, while medical professionals were least likely to be associated with empathy in patient discussions. The findings suggest that online communities addressing the interconnected physical, reproductive, and psychological dimensions of endometriosis fulfill critical gaps in patient support, particularly regarding fertility concerns and life issues discussed within family and partner contexts.

title: Predictors of anxiety in endometriosis patients
summary: Endometriosis patients demonstrate significantly elevated anxiety levels, with distinct clinical and psychological predictors. Ovarian endometriosis and dyspareunia are independently associated with higher trait anxiety, while persistent endometriosis and the need for surgical intervention correlate with increased state anxiety. The chronic inflammation underlying endometriosis appears to alter brain physiology and function, potentially contributing to mood disorders, while the disease's multifaceted symptoms—including pelvic pain, infertility concerns, and sexual dysfunction—create psychological stressors that compound mental health burden through social isolation and partnership difficulties. Treatment approaches matter: hormonal suppression with agents like dienogest reduces both pain and anxiety, whereas incomplete or unsuccessful surgical treatment perpetuates frustration and chronic symptoms. Notably, women with previous pregnancy history show lower anxiety rates, and postoperative hormonal suppression significantly reduces endometriosis recurrence, suggesting that optimized treatment strategies can mitigate both physical symptoms and associated psychological sequelae.

title: The impact of early diagnosis of endometriosis on quality of life
summary: Endometriosis is a chronic gynecological condition characterized by ectopic endometrial tissue that causes significant morbidity through multiple pathways: chronic pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, infertility, and associated mental health complications including depression, anxiety, and stress. The disease substantially impairs quality of life, with advanced-stage endometriosis (stages 3-4) producing greater quality of life impacts than lower stages, and infertility further exacerbating psychological outcomes. While diagnostic timing alone does not significantly differentiate quality of life outcomes between early and late diagnosis, both early diagnosis and timely treatment are critical for alleviating pain symptoms, preventing serious complications such as bowel and ureter involvement, and reducing long-term disease burden. Approximately 74% of endometriosis patients experience misdiagnosis with conditions such as pelvic inflammatory disease, irritable bowel syndrome, or appendicitis, contributing to diagnostic delays. Treatment interventions, particularly surgical approaches, effectively improve quality of life outcomes across both early and late diagnosis groups, though disease severity moderates treatment effectiveness. The economic and financial burden associated with endometriosis management is substantial and comparable to other chronic diseases, underscoring the importance of timely intervention to minimize both clinical morbidity and economic costs.

title: Can the CANKADO online application improve quality of life monitoring via the endometriosis health profile-30 in endometriosis patients: A randomized cohort study on acceptance, usability, and correlations with demographics and media usage
summary: Endometriosis causes multiple severe symptoms including cyclical pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, dysuria, and dyschezia, which substantially impair patients' health-related quality of life across physical, mental, and social domains. This study evaluated the CANKADO online application for monitoring quality of life using the Endometriosis Health Profile-30 (EHP-30), a comprehensive instrument that assesses pain, emotional well-being, control and powerlessness, social support, self-image, work function, sexual intercourse, and infertility concerns. The results demonstrated that over 80% of endometriosis patients showed high acceptance and positive responses to electronic patient-reported outcome assessments via CANKADO, with the online modality rated as more suitable, less complex, and less laborious compared to paper-based versions. Usability correlated strongly with patient demographics and technology habits, as frequent computer use and high IT skills were associated with more positive ratings of suitability and simplified communication, while longer completion times correlated with lower coping ability and greater effort. Electronic PRO assessments via CANKADO were associated with simplified doctor-patient communication, faster diagnosis, and therapeutic improvement while reducing hospitalizations and emergencies. These findings indicate that the CANKADO online application effectively improves health-related quality of life monitoring in endometriosis patients and may enhance therapy monitoring and adherence when properly tailored to patient technology competencies.

title: Pre-operative expectations in patients with endometriosis – a qualitative interview study
summary: This qualitative study explores how pre-operative expectations shape treatment outcomes in endometriosis patients undergoing laparoscopic surgery. Endometriosis produces a diverse symptom burden including dysmenorrhea, pelvic pain, dyspareunia, gastrointestinal symptoms, and infertility, with significant associated mental health consequences such as anxiety and depression. While laparoscopic surgery is recommended for managing endometriosis-related pain and infertility, post-operative outcomes are influenced substantially by patient expectations and biopsychological factors; deep infiltrating endometriosis, concomitant adenomyosis, pain catastrophizing, and negative expectations increase the risk of persistent post-operative symptoms and nocebo effects. Positive treatment outcomes are facilitated by realistic expectations, comprehensive patient information, specialized gynecological care, social support, adequate rest, anti-inflammatory diet, and continued professional supervision. Conversely, poor post-operative quality of life is hindered by post-operative pain, scarring, insufficient rest, stress, and inadequate social or professional support. The study emphasizes that optimizing post-operative outcomes requires multifaceted approaches combining informed decision-making, psychological support, and robust social networks rather than surgery alone.

title: Patterns of Associations with Epidemiologic Factors by High-Grade Serous Ovarian Cancer Gene Expression Subtypes
summary: This study examined how epidemiologic risk factors associate with distinct molecular subtypes of high-grade serous ovarian cancer (HGSC). Endometriosis showed moderate associations with two HGSC subtypes: C5.PRO (OR = 1.46) and C4.DIF (OR = 1.27), suggesting that endometriosis may predispose to specific molecular phenotypes of ovarian cancer. Environmental exposures and reproductive factors demonstrated differential associations across HGSC subtypes, with former smoking and genital powder use strongly associated with the C2.IMM subtype, while family history of breast cancer and current smoking were associated with C4.DIF. Protective and reproductive factors including oral contraceptive use, parity, breastfeeding, and family history of ovarian cancer showed consistent associations across HGSC molecular subtypes. These findings indicate that epidemiologic risk factors for ovarian cancer are not uniformly distributed across molecular subtypes, suggesting distinct etiologic pathways may underlie different HGSC phenotypes.

title: Insights into endometriosis symptom trajectories and assessment of surgical intervention outcomes using longitudinal actigraphy
summary:
# Endometriosis Symptom Trajectories and Surgical Outcomes

This study used longitudinal actigraphy to characterize endometriosis symptom trajectories and assess surgical intervention outcomes, revealing that endometriosis presents with highly heterogeneous manifestations including debilitating pain, fatigue, sleep disturbance, and reduced physical activity. The research found strong correlations between daily pain and fatigue levels, with symptom severity directly associated with decreased physical activity and disrupted sleep patterns, indicating that fatigue impacts emotional and social functioning more than pain alone. Notably, patients with cyclical symptom flares demonstrated measurable changes in sleep duration and regularity, while those with consistently severe symptoms showed persistently lower activity levels and variable sleep despite treatment. Both surgical intervention and pharmacological treatment with GnRH agonists resulted in detectable improvements in physical activity levels and sleep patterns that aligned with subjective symptom reduction, demonstrating that actigraphy captures meaningful biological changes following endometriosis treatment. This longitudinal approach using wrist-worn accelerometers provides objective assessment of endometriosis impact on sleep and activity that extends beyond traditional questionnaire-based measures, offering potential for improved monitoring of treatment response in this chronic condition.

title: Endo Belly: A Mixed Methods Exploration of Body Image, Disordered Eating, and Psychopathology in Endometriosis
summary: This mixed methods study reveals that endometriosis creates a complex psychological burden beyond its well-established physical symptoms of pain, bloating, infertility, fatigue, and sexual dysfunction. Individuals with endometriosis demonstrate significantly elevated eating disorder psychopathology, negative affect, and self-criticism alongside reduced body image flexibility compared to normative samples, driven partly by visible physical changes including surgical scarring and weight gain difficulties. The research identifies a critical pathway wherein body image dissatisfaction and negative affect directly promote disordered eating behaviors, with higher pain levels and BMI further exacerbating these patterns. Qualitative findings highlight three interconnected psychological themes—body disappointment, viewing food as an enemy, and loss of identity and joy—that underscore how endometriosis-related physical changes translate into maladaptive eating attitudes and depressive symptoms mediated by self-criticism and body image concerns. These findings underscore that endometriosis-related psychological distress, including anxiety and depression, operates through body image concerns and self-directed criticism, creating a bidirectional relationship between physical symptoms and mental health outcomes. The study emphasizes the need for integrated mental health interventions addressing eating behaviors and body image flexibility as part of comprehensive endometriosis care.

title: Impaired gastric and urinary but preserved cardiac interoception in women with endometriosis
summary:
# Summary

This study examined interoceptive abilities—the capacity to perceive internal bodily signals—in women with endometriosis compared to healthy controls. Women with endometriosis, who suffer from chronic pelvic pain including dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and dyschezia, demonstrated significantly impaired gastric and urinary interoception as measured by the Water Load Test and Urinary Interoceptive Task, with elevated subjective bladder beliefs despite objective sensory deficits. Notably, cardiac interoception assessed via the Heartbeat Counting Task remained preserved in the endometriosis group, suggesting visceral hypersensitivity and altered interoceptive processing are organ-specific rather than global. The authors propose that pain-related hypervigilance and the shared parasympathetic neural pathways of the stomach and bladder may account for these domain-specific impairments, distinct from the sympathetic cardiac pathway. Additionally, women with endometriosis showed elevated depersonalization and body image disturbance scores, consistent with broader disruptions in interoceptive self-regulation that may mediate the relationship between chronic pain and concurrent anxiety and depression. These findings suggest that impaired visceral interoception could be both a consequence of and contributor to the pain pathophysiology in endometriosis.

title: Virtual reality-based pain control in endometriosis: a questionnaire-based pilot study of applications for relaxation and physical activity
summary: Endometriosis, characterized by ectopic growth of endometrial cells throughout the pelvis and beyond, causes chronic pain symptoms including dysmenorrhea and dyspareunia, and is strongly associated with psychological comorbidities including depression, anxiety, and sleep disorders. This pilot study evaluated virtual reality interventions—one focused on relaxation (VR-R) and one on physical activity (VR-A)—as novel pain management strategies for endometriosis patients. VR-R demonstrated significantly greater effects than control conditions across multiple outcome measures, substantially reducing pain intensity and pain catastrophizing while improving sleep quality, depression, stress, and helplessness. VR-A also showed improvements in pain disability, catastrophizing, depression, and stress, though these gains were not significantly greater than controls. The findings suggest that virtual reality-based interventions, particularly relaxation-focused applications, offer promise as adjunctive non-pharmacological treatments for managing the multifaceted burden of endometriosis-related pain and associated mental health symptoms.

title: Outcomes Following Surgery As the Main Intervention for Endometriosis in a Low Socioeconomic Setting
journal: JSLS : Journal of the Society of Laparoscopic & Robotic Surgeons
summary: This study evaluates surgical outcomes for endometriosis treatment in a resource-limited setting, demonstrating that surgical management effectively reduces the pain and infertility burden associated with the disease. Among 38 patients undergoing surgery, multiple approaches were employed including laparoscopy (59%), robotic-assisted surgery (33.3%), and laparotomy, with advanced techniques such as ureterolysis, enterolysis, bowel resection, and partial bladder cystectomy utilized for complex cases. Notably, 16 of 19 patients who regularly used pain medication before surgery discontinued their pain medications postoperatively, indicating substantial symptom relief. Regarding fertility outcomes, one patient achieved spontaneous conception following surgery, while eight patients actively continued conception attempts. Postoperatively, 16 patients received hormonal suppression therapy, representing an integrated treatment approach combining surgery with medical management. These findings underscore that comprehensive surgical intervention for endometriosis, including advanced techniques for deeply infiltrating disease, achieves significant clinical benefits in pain reduction and fertility preservation even in low-resource healthcare settings.

title: The impact of reproductive health needs on women’s employment: a qualitative insight into managing endometriosis and work
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is a debilitating reproductive condition closely associated with infertility and a broad spectrum of physical symptoms including chronic pelvic pain, painful periods, nerve and joint pain, bowel problems, migraines, fatigue, brain fog, and poor sleep, all of which substantially reduce quality of life. Beyond physical manifestations, the condition causes significant psychological distress, depressive symptoms, and emotional difficulty, with infertility-related impacts and fertility treatments like in-vitro fertilization adding additional physical and psychological burdens. Women with endometriosis face substantial workplace challenges, experiencing difficulty managing symptoms at work, impaired career progression, and emotional distress related to employment performance and pressures. Flexible work arrangements including remote work options, supportive leave policies, heat therapy, and pain management medications like Tramadol help mitigate symptom burden and improve both symptom control and emotional wellbeing in the workplace. This qualitative evidence underscores that endometriosis and associated reproductive health conditions require comprehensive workplace accommodations and supportive policies to enable women's continued employment and mental health stability.

title: Fertility in Celiac Disease: The Impact of Gluten on Male and Female Reproductive Health
summary:
# Summary

Celiac disease, an immune-mediated enteropathy triggered by gluten ingestion, significantly impacts fertility in both men and women through multiple biological mechanisms. In women, untreated celiac disease causes intestinal villous atrophy and nutritional deficiencies that contribute to unexplained infertility, primary ovarian insufficiency, and adverse pregnancy outcomes including spontaneous abortion, stillbirth, and miscarriage. The disease also disrupts reproductive endocrinology by reducing anti-Müllerian hormone levels, increasing prolactin, and triggering menstrual abnormalities, polycystic ovarian syndrome, and primary ovarian failure. In men, celiac disease-induced deficiencies in zinc and selenium, combined with increased oxidative stress, impair sperm development and function. Importantly, serological screening for tissue transglutaminase and endomysium antibodies can identify undiagnosed celiac disease in infertile populations, as evidence demonstrates that women with positive celiac serology have significantly higher rates of unexplained infertility. Adoption of a gluten-free diet can improve fertility outcomes, making celiac disease screening a valuable intervention for infertile individuals, particularly those with unexplained cases.

title: Self-inflicted chronic laparoscopic wounds in endometriosis—unveiling major depressive disorder: a case report
summary: This case report documents a patient with endometriosis who developed self-inflicted laparoscopic wounds as a manifestation of major depressive disorder, illustrating the critical bidirectional relationship between endometriosis and mental health. Endometriosis causes multiple debilitating symptoms including dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, infertility, and chronic pelvic pain that collectively impair quality of life and contribute to depression and anxiety through mechanisms involving neuroinflammation and central sensitization. The patient's depressive symptoms, exacerbated by delayed diagnosis and inadequate response to various endometriosis treatments (progestin-only pill and dienogest), escalated to self-directed violence, highlighting that psychiatric comorbidities in endometriosis patients represent a serious clinical concern requiring integrated care. Treatment with the selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor fluoxetine remarkably improved both depressive symptoms and the chronic postlaparoscopic wounds, suggesting that addressing mental health is essential for managing the full spectrum of endometriosis-related pathology. The case emphasizes that endometriosis patients should be screened for anxiety, depression, posttraumatic stress disorder, and eating disorders, and that delayed diagnosis significantly increases the risk of developing severe mental health complications in adolescent and young patients. Long-term management with hormonal therapies (Mirena, leuprolide with add-back) combined with psychiatric support represents appropriate multimodal treatment for this complex, life-altering condition.

title: Embodied distress in reproductive psychiatry
summary: Endometriosis, a prevalent gynecological condition affecting women of reproductive age, causes chronic pain that significantly impairs quality of life. The condition is strongly associated with infertility, a well-documented relationship in the clinical literature. Beyond the physical manifestations of pain and reproductive dysfunction, endometriosis creates substantial psychological burden through embodied distress—the experiential integration of physical symptoms with mental health consequences. This intersection of chronic pain, fertility challenges, and psychiatric symptoms requires an integrated reproductive psychiatry approach that addresses both somatic and psychological dimensions of the disease. The chronic nature of endometriosis-related pain perpetuates cycles of distress that can exacerbate depression, anxiety, and other mental health complications, highlighting the need for comprehensive biopsychosocial management strategies in clinical care.

title: Prevalence and Associated Factors of Infertility among Married Couples of Kunnathukal Panchayath in Trivandrum District
journal: Indian Journal of Community Medicine: Official Publication of Indian Association of Preventive & Social Medicine
summary:
# Summary

This community-based study identified multiple factors associated with infertility among married couples in Kerala, India. Dysmenorrhea and its type emerged as significant predictors of infertility, along with uterine fibroids, which were substantially more prevalent in infertile women (3.9–6%) compared to fertile women (1.3–3%). Other reproductive and systemic factors associated with infertility included dyspareunia, hypothyroidism, higher BMI, later age at menarche, and female occupation, while oligozoospermia and polycystic ovary syndrome were the most common abnormalities in affected males. Notably, conditions such as anemia, tuberculosis, pelvic inflammatory disease, and endometriosis showed no statistically significant association with infertility in this population, suggesting variable regional or population-specific patterns in reproductive health outcomes. The findings indicate that dysmenorrhea-related symptoms and structural uterine pathology are key modifiable factors warranting clinical attention in infertility management.

title: Factors associated with female infertility in Ethiopia: A systematic review and meta-analysis
summary: This systematic review and meta-analysis identified multiple behavioral, infectious, and biological factors contributing to female infertility in Ethiopia. Sexually transmitted infections, particularly chlamydia and gonorrhea, emerged as major risk factors, with untreated STDs progressing to pelvic inflammatory disease and subsequent fallopian tube obstruction. Behavioral and partner-related risk factors included multiple sexual partners (4.31-fold increased risk), STD history (2.76-fold increased risk), and partners with alcohol or khat abuse (1.57- to 1.85-fold increased risk), while advanced maternal age over 35 years increased risk 2.07-fold. Reproductive tract pathologies such as polycystic ovary syndrome, endometritis, fallopian tube obstruction, and hormonal imbalances were also significantly associated with infertility. Beyond the direct biological consequences, the review highlighted substantial psychological and social impacts of infertility, including marital instability, depression, and anxiety, underscoring the need for comprehensive management addressing both fertility and mental health outcomes.

title: “Take me seriously”: A qualitative interview study exploring healthcare experiences of endometriosis patients
summary: Endometriosis is a chronic condition that causes multiple debilitating symptoms including pain, bladder and bowel dysfunction, painful intercourse, and infertility, while also increasing anxiety and substantially reducing quality of life. The disease is frequently associated with delayed diagnosis and stigmatization, partly due to systemic healthcare limitations and lack of provider awareness. A critical finding from patient interviews is that healthcare provider communication and empathy significantly influence diagnostic speed and patient outcomes; supportive, empathetic providers who take time to listen accelerate diagnosis, whereas dismissive communication and lack of empathy perpetuate diagnostic delays and poor patient experiences. Current treatment approaches include oral contraceptives, painkillers, opioids, and plant-based remedies like Vitex agnus-castus, though these typically address symptoms rather than underlying disease. Patients emphasized that detailed education about their condition and empathetic provider communication are the most important factors in achieving positive healthcare experiences. The research underscores that improving endometriosis care requires not only greater healthcare system awareness and specialized care access, but also fundamental changes in provider-patient communication and validation of patient experiences.

title: What is needed to improve young people's access to sexual health care through primary care?
summary: This article examines barriers to sexual health care access among young people aged 15–29 years in Australia, a population experiencing high rates of sexually transmitted infections and reproductive health complications. The study identifies that risk behaviors common to adolescence and early adulthood, particularly condomless sex, significantly contribute to chlamydia infection and unintended pregnancy, with untreated infections causing potentially irreversible infertility in women. Beyond infections, primary care practitioners manage related reproductive health issues including endometriosis-associated pelvic pain in young patients. Multiple structural and social barriers impede care access, including high costs, long waiting times, transportation limitations, and pervasive societal stigmatization of sexual health. The article emphasizes that improving young people's sexual health outcomes requires addressing these systemic obstacles through enhanced primary care infrastructure and reduced stigma surrounding sexual health discussions.
