title: Impact of Therapeutic Interventions on Fear of Progression in Patients with Endometriosis
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis causes chronic pelvic pain and infertility while being strongly associated with anxiety and depression, conditions further exacerbated by dysregulation of neuroendocrine pathways that create a vicious cycle between pain perception and mental stress. Fear of progression in endometriosis patients emerges as a significant clinical concern, particularly contributing to insomnia, fatigue, and sleep disturbances, with notable demographic and treatment-related variations. Younger patients and those receiving exclusive hormonal therapy demonstrated the highest fear of progression scores, while patients undergoing surgical intervention or those with a definitive surgical plan showed significantly lower anxiety about disease progression, suggesting that the perception of a "solution" substantially reduces psychological burden. Key patient concerns centered on fear of medical procedures and body image impacts rather than heredity considerations. A multidisciplinary management approach incorporating psychological counseling, cognitive-behavioral therapy, mind-body techniques such as yoga and mindfulness meditation, and effective clinician communication regarding treatment rationale substantially reduces fear of progression and improves patient outcomes. Surgical treatments including laparoscopic excision, ablation, cystectomy, and adhesiolysis directly address the underlying pathology, while hormonal therapy alone provides only disease suppression without definitive resolution, potentially explaining the differential psychological burden across treatment modalities.

title: Unmet Needs of Endometriosis Patients with Respect to Health Care Services: A Qualitative Study Using a Patient Training Workshop
summary: Endometriosis affects up to 10% of women of reproductive age and functions as a chronic inflammatory disease with substantial impacts on patient quality of life, causing pain, infertility, and emotional burden while frequently associated with delayed diagnosis. This qualitative study employed a co-production approach with endometriosis patients through a training workshop to systematically identify unmet healthcare needs, overcoming barriers such as patient beliefs and educational limitations that typically impede participation in service design. The analysis revealed that patient priorities vary significantly by age and time since diagnosis: younger patients (under 35 years) prioritize information about fertility, sexual health, and disease understanding, while older patients seek improved access to specialist consultations and multidisciplinary care. The patient training workshop successfully improved participation capacity with high satisfaction ratings (83%) and facilitated identification of specific, actionable unmet needs that can drive targeted healthcare improvements. This co-production methodology represents a novel and effective strategy for endometriosis care redesign, demonstrating that incorporating patient perspectives in healthcare service development leads to identification of unmet needs and enables design of truly patient-centered interventions.

title: Endometriosis, Raynaud’s Syndrome, and Migraine: A Retrospective Study of 12 Million Women
summary:
# Summary

This retrospective study of 12 million women found strong associations between endometriosis and two vascular/neurological conditions: Raynaud's syndrome and migraine, with unadjusted odds ratios of approximately 2.49, and even stronger associations in younger women aged 18–35. Endometriosis, characterized by chronic inflammatory endometrial-like tissue outside the uterus, appears to share common pathophysiological mechanisms with these conditions involving vascular dysfunction and endothelial dysfunction. The study also identified significant mental health comorbidities, with endometriosis associated with depression and anxiety, and both migraine and Raynaud's syndrome showing strong associations with these psychiatric disorders. Notably, migraine and Raynaud's syndrome themselves were significantly correlated, and all three conditions clustered together with anxiety and depression, suggesting a shared underlying inflammatory or vascular mechanism. The findings suggest that women with endometriosis should be screened for migraine, Raynaud's syndrome, and mental health disorders, as these conditions may reflect a common pathophysiological pathway related to chronic inflammation and vascular dysfunction.

title: Association between endometriosis and type and age of menopause: a pooled analysis of 279 948 women from five cohort studies
summary: This large pooled analysis of nearly 280,000 women demonstrates that endometriosis substantially increases the risk of premature and early menopause, particularly surgical menopause, with affected women being seven times more likely to undergo bilateral oophorectomy and experiencing surgical menopause approximately 19 months earlier than women without the condition. The association is mediated partly by infertility and nulliparity, suggesting shared reproductive risk factors, though direct biological mechanisms including reduced ovarian reserve, peritoneal inflammation, immune dysfunction, and pelvic adhesions also contribute to earlier menopause in endometriosis patients. Women with endometriosis show markers of diminished ovarian reserve including lower anti-Müllerian hormone and antral follicle counts, with surgical treatment further reducing these markers by up to 40 percent. The condition is associated with multiple comorbidities including autoimmune disorders, thyroid disease, and a nearly twofold increased ovarian cancer risk driven primarily by endometriomas. Treatment options include conservative approaches such as oral contraceptives, progestins, and lesion excision or ablation, while women undergoing surgical menopause require combined estrogen-progestogen therapy rather than estrogen-only therapy due to recurrence and malignant transformation risks in those with endometriosis history.

title: Examining the relationship between endometriosis and psychological distress: roles of cultural background, body image and self-criticism in women's health
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis causes multifaceted physical symptoms including chronic pelvic pain, fatigue, dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and infertility, which collectively contribute to elevated psychological distress, depression, and anxiety in affected women. The relationship between endometriosis and psychological distress is substantially mediated by body image dissatisfaction and self-criticism: endometriosis-related physical changes such as weight fluctuations from hormonal treatments lead to lower body appreciation and increased self-criticism, which in turn intensify depressive and anxious symptoms. Cultural background significantly moderates these associations, with Israeli women with endometriosis experiencing particularly pronounced adverse effects on depression and anxiety, potentially driven by pronatalist societal contexts that heighten infertility-related stigma and psychological burden. The study identifies a self-perpetuating cycle in which societal body ideals contribute to body dissatisfaction and self-critical cognitions, generating emotions of shame and guilt that further erode self-esteem and compound mental health difficulties. These findings suggest that comprehensive clinical management of endometriosis should address psychological factors including body image concerns and maladaptive self-criticism patterns alongside physical symptom management to improve overall health-related quality of life.

title: Global and regional trends in the burden of surgically confirmed endometriosis from 1990 to 2021
summary: This global epidemiological analysis reveals that endometriosis imposes a substantial disease burden driven primarily by population growth rather than age-standardized rate increases. Surgically confirmed endometriosis predominantly affects women aged 20-30 years and is associated with multiple serious complications including infertility, chronic pelvic discomfort, depression, and anxiety, reflecting both the inflammatory pathophysiology and significant psychosocial impact of the disease. Notably, age-standardized incidence, prevalence, and years lived with disability from endometriosis decline with increasing sociodemographic development index, suggesting that higher-SDI regions experience lower disease burden despite potentially higher diagnostic detection capabilities. The study underscores endometriosis as a major gynecological condition with substantial reproductive and mental health consequences, particularly in younger women, with population dynamics serving as the primary global driver of case increases from 1990 to 2021.

title: An insect brain-based bioelectronic neural sensor for the systemic detection and precise classification of endometriosis
summary: Endometriosis, a chronic inflammatory disease with significant diagnostic challenges, is associated with distinct metabolic differences that manifest as unique volatile organic compound (VOC) profiles. Both endometriotic and endometrial cell lines produce characteristic VOCs that can be differentiated using a novel bioelectronic neural sensor based on insect brain tissue. This sensor-based approach leverages the metabolic distinctions between endometrial and endometriotic cells to enable systemic detection and precise classification of endometriosis through analysis of emitted gases. The study demonstrates that volatile organic compound profiling represents a viable biomarker strategy for identifying endometriosis, potentially addressing the current lack of non-invasive screening options and the documented diagnostic delays associated with the condition. By translating insect neural recognition capabilities into a bioelectronic platform, the authors provide a proof-of-concept for rapid, objective endometriosis detection based on cellular metabolic signatures.

title: A Template Thematic Analysis of Self‐Concept and Self‐Compassion in People Living With Endometriosis: Analysis of Qualitative Survey Responses
summary:
# Summary

This qualitative study reveals that endometriosis profoundly alters self-concept across personal, social, and physical domains, generating substantial psychological consequences including self-blame, inadequacy, isolation, and identity disturbance marked by loss of female identity and disrupted motherhood aspirations. The condition's physical manifestations—chronic pain, fatigue, bloating, weight gain, and sexual dysfunction—combined with infertility and social role disruptions create a negative feedback loop where altered self-concept reinforces isolation, emotional suppression, and self-silencing. Self-compassion, comprising self-kindness, common humanity, and mindfulness, emerges as a protective mechanism that may buffer against these psychological harms by reducing self-judgment and combating social isolation. Healthcare providers are encouraged to deliver compassion-focused, interdisciplinary care emphasizing timely diagnosis and therapeutic approaches that foster self-acceptance and recognition of shared experiences among individuals living with endometriosis.

title: Interaction between genetic risk and comorbid conditions in endometriosis
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis, characterized by endometrial-like tissue growth outside the uterus, exhibits significant interactions between polygenic risk and multiple comorbid conditions. This study analyzed data from the UK Biobank and Estonian Biobank to demonstrate that endometriosis cases carry higher polygenic risk scores (PRS) and greater comorbidity burden than controls, with endometriosis strongly associated with uterine fibroids, dysmenorrhea, heavy menstrual bleeding, irritable bowel syndrome, and diverticular disease. Notably, an additive interactive effect was observed between endometriosis PRS and comorbid conditions like uterine fibroids, suggesting that genetic risk and comorbid diagnoses together elevate endometriosis susceptibility more substantially than either factor alone. While genome-wide association studies identified 49 significant endometriosis variants explaining 1.98% of disease variance, the endometriosis PRS alone shows limited predictive utility in isolation. The findings highlight that comorbidities complicate endometriosis diagnosis through symptom overlap and affect patient quality of life, and suggest that integrating both polygenic risk and comorbid disease status may improve risk stratification and clinical management of this complex gynecological condition.

title: Assessment of Endometriosis Knowledge and Its Determinants Among Nurses in Al-Jouf Region, Saudi Arabia
summary: Endometriosis is a chronic, estrogen-dependent gynecological disorder characterized by endometrial-like tissue growth outside the uterine cavity, causing multiple clinical manifestations including chronic pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, infertility, dyspareunia, menorrhagia, dyschezia, and painful bladder syndrome, while also contributing to depression and reduced work productivity. Risk factors associated with endometriosis development include early menarche, late menopause, low body mass index, and family history. Early recognition of endometriosis symptoms by nurses is critical for timely intervention and improved patient outcomes; however, this study found that 61% of nurses in the Al-Jouf region of Saudi Arabia scored below 60% on endometriosis knowledge assessments, indicating a significant knowledge gap. Educational attainment emerged as the strongest predictor of endometriosis knowledge among nurses (β = 0.415), followed by age, years of clinical experience, and direct patient care exposure, suggesting that continued professional development and clinical engagement are essential for enhancing nurses' ability to identify and manage this condition effectively.

title: Quality of Life in Women with Endometriosis: The Importance of Socio-Demographic, Diagnostic-Therapeutic, and Psychological Factors
summary: Endometriosis significantly reduces quality of life through multiple mechanisms, causing chronic pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and infertility while triggering fibrosis and adhesion development at the tissue level. The disease also generates substantial psychological burdens, including anxiety, depression, reduced self-esteem, irritability, helplessness, and social deterioration including isolation and stigmatization. Notably, psychological factors emerge as critical protective mechanisms: higher self-efficacy and dispositional optimism independently predict better overall quality of life across pain, emotional well-being, social support, sexual functioning, and treatment satisfaction domains. Diagnostic-therapeutic factors significantly impact outcomes, with consultation of more physicians before diagnosis and perceived economic burden of treatment both associated with lower quality of life across multiple domains. Age and education demonstrate complex associations, with younger women and those with higher education reporting lower quality of life, potentially reflecting stronger social role pressures and higher treatment expectations, respectively, while infertility-related outcomes worsen substantially in women without motherhood experience. The findings support integrating psychological resources and a biopsychosocial framework into personalized endometriosis management strategies.

title: Comprehensive Approaches to Endometriosis Management
summary:
# Summary

This comprehensive review examines endometriosis as a multisystem disease with far-reaching physiological and psychological consequences. Beyond its hallmark association with chronic pelvic pain, endometriosis causes significant social impairment and mental health disorders, and evidence indicates it induces distinct neurological changes termed "endometriosis brain." The condition shows robust epidemiological links with multiple autoimmune diseases including systemic lupus erythematosus, rheumatoid arthritis, Sjögren's syndrome, and inflammatory bowel disease, with elevated regulatory T cells identified as a shared immune feature. Endometriosis-related infertility stems from multiple mechanisms including altered pelvic anatomy, disrupted ovarian reserve, and compromised endometrial receptivity, with endometriosis affecting follicular fluid composition and markers like interleukin-6 and anti-müllerian hormone. Adjuvant hormonal therapy with dienogest significantly reduces pain symptoms following surgery, while adenomyosis coexistence substantially diminishes clinical pregnancy and live birth rates while increasing miscarriage risk in assisted reproductive procedures.

title: To operate or not to operate? The impact of surgical treatment on quality of life in women with ovarian endometriosis
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis, particularly ovarian and deep endometriosis subtypes, substantially reduces quality of life by causing chronic pain, infertility, sexual dysfunction, and psychological distress including depression and anxiety. Women with deep endometriosis and non-surgically treated ovarian endometriosis experience the most severe impairments, demonstrating significantly lower physical and mental health scores, reduced vitality, increased pain sensitivity, impaired work and social functioning, and diminished self-esteem compared to controls. Notably, surgical treatment preserves and restores vital functions including sexual, reproductive, and occupational capacity, with surgically managed ovarian endometriosis patients showing intermediate health outcomes between controls and non-surgically treated patients. The multifactorial nature of endometriosis burden is compounded by delayed diagnosis, pain catastrophizing, social isolation, and sleep disturbances, underscoring the need for multidisciplinary treatment approaches combining pain management, psychological support, and surgical intervention when appropriate to optimize quality of life outcomes.

title: Psychosocial aspects of infertility and the impact of assisted reproductive
summary:
# Summary

Infertility and associated gynecological conditions such as endometriosis, polycystic ovary syndrome, and premature ovarian insufficiency impose substantial psychological burdens on affected individuals, commonly triggering anxiety, depression, poor self-esteem, and distress. While assisted reproductive technologies including in vitro fertilization, intrauterine insemination, and surrogacy effectively treat infertility, these treatments themselves generate significant psychological stress and financial difficulties for patients. Innovative approaches such as the ROPA method for female couples and gestational surrogacy for women with absent or compromised uteri offer clinical solutions while potentially mitigating psychological harm—the ROPA method particularly reduces feelings of exclusion and jealousy by allowing both partners to assume maternal roles. However, alternative reproductive pathways including gamete and embryo donation introduce additional anxiety, while commercial surrogacy raises ethical concerns regarding pressure on gestational carriers. Overall, the psychosocial dimensions of infertility treatment demand integrated clinical support addressing both the physiological and mental health needs of individuals and couples navigating these complex reproductive decisions.

title: Perception of fertility, quality of life, and depression in women
summary: This study examined the interconnected relationships between depression, fertility-related quality of life, and infertility in women undergoing assisted reproductive treatment. Depression was strongly associated with significantly lower fertility-related quality of life across multiple dimensions, including social relationships, treatment environment, and mind-body aspects, with depressed patients scoring 11 to 23 points lower on the FertiQoL scale. Multiple infertility etiologies were identified, including endometriosis, pelvic inflammatory disease, ovulation disorders, and polycystic ovary syndrome, which are managed through assisted reproductive techniques such as in vitro fertilization and intracytoplasmic sperm injection. Women with secondary infertility and prolonged infertility duration showed distinct quality-of-life patterns, with prolonged infertility associated with lower mind-body, social, and tolerability domain scores. Notably, patients seeking psychological support reported lower quality-of-life scores across most domains, suggesting either that more severely affected individuals seek help or that current psychological interventions may need optimization. Educational attainment showed a counterintuitive positive association with depression, while positive conjugal and sexual relationships correlated with better fertility-related quality of life outcomes.

title: Endometriosis– “Either way a tragedy”? A qualitative social media analysis of endometriosis perceptions in Germany
summary: This qualitative social media analysis reveals endometriosis as a multifaceted disease with profound physical and psychological consequences for German patients. Endometriosis causes chronic pelvic pain, painful menstruation, dyspareunia, and fertility problems through inflammatory mechanisms and fallopian tube adhesions, with severe cases potentially leading to organ damage and even suicidal ideation. Beyond physical symptoms, the condition triggers significant psychological distress including depression, anxiety, post-traumatic stress-like symptoms, and social isolation, compounded by loss of quality of life, workplace difficulties, and relationship strain. A critical finding is that systemic barriers—including gender bias in medical research and healthcare, insufficient provider knowledge, societal stigma around menstrual pain, and lack of specialized endometriosis centers—contribute to diagnostic delays and misdiagnoses that intensify patient suffering through medical gaslighting and invalidation. While conventional hormonal treatments and emerging digital health applications like the Endo-App offer some management options, patients increasingly turn to complementary approaches and peer support networks on social media, though these platforms risk spreading misinformation. The analysis underscores that endometriosis requires integrated care addressing both organic pathology and the profound psychosocial burden created by diagnostic delays and systemic medical neglect.

title: ‘Listen to women as if they were your most cherished person’: Australian women’s perspectives on living with the pain of endometriosis: A mixed-methods study
summary: This mixed-methods study reveals that endometriosis causes extensive physical, psychological, and social burden in Australian women, with debilitating pain, heavy bleeding, fatigue, and painful intercourse triggering substantial reductions in quality of life, work capacity, and social participation. The psychological toll is considerable, with 50-86% of women experiencing depression, anxiety, and stress, while fatigue affects over 70% and significantly impairs daily functioning. A critical barrier to care emerges from societal stigma surrounding menstrual symptoms, which delays diagnosis, promotes symptom concealment, and forces women into avoidance of help-seeking behavior; this problem is compounded by inadequate healthcare provider knowledge and dismissal, leading to diagnostic delay, misdiagnosis, and financial burden from "doctor shopping." Women emphasize that practitioners must recognize endometriosis as a pain-centered condition requiring clinician empathy and competence, and that self-education, self-advocacy, and social support are essential mechanisms for self-empowerment and improved outcomes. The research underscores that addressing endometriosis management requires shifting from a reductionist biomedical approach to one that centers on individual pain experiences and develops patient autonomy.

title: Assessing the knowledge, attitudes, and perceptions of ovarian cryopreservation technology among women with ovarian diseases
summary:
# Summary

Ovarian diseases, including endometriosis and polycystic ovary syndrome, significantly impact women's reproductive health by causing infertility, difficulty conceiving, irregular menstrual cycles, and pain, with 37.6% reporting substantial reductions in quality of life. Both oocyte cryopreservation and ovarian tissue cryopreservation offer therapeutic potential to preserve fertility and restore ovarian function in women facing these conditions or premature ovarian insufficiency caused by cancer treatments or genetic factors. Women's knowledge, attitudes, and perceptions regarding cryopreservation technology are strongly correlated with each other, and higher educational attainment, urban residence, and greater income are associated with improved understanding and more positive attitudes toward these fertility preservation options. However, significant barriers to adoption exist, including the high cost of treatment, which 78.1% of participants identified as prohibitive, as well as cultural and religious factors that influence decision-making. Educational interventions targeting women with ovarian diseases should prioritize equitable access across socioeconomic and geographic populations to ensure informed decision-making about fertility preservation technologies.

title: Can self-compassion and mindfulness predict psychological wellbeing in individuals with endometriosis? Findings from an online survey
summary: Endometriosis causes multiple overlapping symptoms including dysmenorrhea, non-menstrual pelvic pain, dyspareunia, dyschezia, dysuria, infertility, chronic fatigue, and sleep disorders, while frequently co-occurring with anxiety, depression, and other chronic pain conditions such as fibromyalgia and vulvodynia. This study examined whether self-compassion and mindfulness could predict psychological wellbeing in individuals with endometriosis, finding that both constructs showed positive associations with psychological wellbeing. Self-compassion emerged as a particularly important protective factor, reducing symptoms of anxiety and depression while improving health-related quality of life, sexual distress, and stress-related inflammation. Notably, pain severity and symptom burden were associated with lower self-compassion, highlighting how the disease burden impairs protective psychological resources. Mindfulness-based interventions demonstrated therapeutic potential by reducing pain and improving psychological wellbeing through enhanced parasympathetic regulation and attention. These findings suggest that psychological interventions targeting self-compassion and mindfulness may offer valuable complementary approaches for managing both the physical and mental health burden of endometriosis.

title: Dietary factors and risk for endometriosis: a Mendelian randomization analysis
summary: This Mendelian randomization study investigated the causal relationships between dietary factors and endometriosis risk, a chronic inflammatory condition characterized by severe pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, and infertility. Endometriosis pathogenesis involves complex interactions among immune imbalance, hormonal alterations, and inflammation, processes in which diet plays a modulatory role through effects on oxidative stress, inflammatory pathways, and steroid hormone metabolism. The analysis identified salad and raw vegetable intake as protective factors against endometriosis, reducing risk by approximately 65%, while processed meat intake also showed a protective association. Conversely, a dietary pattern characterized by high red meat and trans fat consumption was associated with increased endometriosis risk, whereas consumption of healthier dietary patterns rich in fruits and vegetables was protective. Many commonly studied foods including poultry, fish, dairy, alcohol, coffee, and tea showed no significant association with endometriosis risk in this analysis. These findings suggest that dietary interventions emphasizing plant-based foods while limiting processed and red meat may represent a modifiable strategy for reducing endometriosis risk.

title: Association between age at menarche and infertility in Tabari cohort population: a population-based case-control study
summary: This population-based case-control study demonstrates that early and normative ages at menarche are significantly associated with increased infertility risk compared to late menarche, with women experiencing early menarche showing 3.36-fold higher odds of infertility. Early menarche increases infertility risk through multiple reproductive pathways, including increased susceptibility to polycystic ovary syndrome and endometriosis, reduced ovarian function, accelerated oocyte depletion, irregular menstrual cycles, and higher miscarriage rates. Additionally, early menarche is associated with premature or early menopause, which further shortens the reproductive window. The protective effect of late menarche against infertility and the higher infertility rates observed in women with lower oral contraceptive use and higher abortion histories suggest that both the timing of reproductive events and reproductive health management influence fertility outcomes. These findings underscore how the age at menarche fundamentally shapes long-term reproductive health trajectories and identify oral contraceptive use as a potentially protective factor against infertility-associated conditions.

title: Understanding the Role of Social Negativity in Perceived Life Course Impact and Mental Health Among Women with Endometriosis
summary: Endometriosis, a chronic inflammatory gynecological disease, causes multiple debilitating symptoms including chronic pelvic pain, dyspareunia, painful menstruation, and infertility, which collectively exert significant negative impacts on women's perceived life course across intimate relationships, employment, and education. Beyond physical symptoms, women with endometriosis experience elevated rates of anxiety and depression alongside compromised psychosocial well-being, effects amplified by social negativity and reduced social support. Social negativity emerged as a strong predictor of negative perceived life course impact, while menstrual pain, social support, and healthcare accessibility were identified as significant independent predictors of both life course impact and mental health outcomes. The study underscores that infertility complications further burden mental health and social relationships, with dyspareunia and chronic pain directly harming women's sexuality and self-esteem. Interventions addressing social support, healthcare access, and lifestyle modifications—including dietary adjustments and regular exercise to reduce estrogen levels—alongside symptom management may mitigate the disease's psychosocial burden and improve mental health outcomes in this population.

title: Nutrition Intervention in Gynecological Diseases
summary:
# Summary

This review demonstrates that dietary and nutritional interventions play a critical role in managing gynecological diseases including endometriosis, polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS), uterine fibroids, and dysmenorrhea. Endometriosis, a chronic inflammatory condition, causes chronic pain and infertility through mechanisms involving diet-induced inflammation and oxidative stress, which can be mitigated through polyphenol-rich, anti-inflammatory, Mediterranean, and low-FODMAP diets supplemented with vitamins C, D, and E, polyunsaturated fatty acids, and iron. Poor dietary choices trigger chronic inflammation underlying these conditions, while nutrient-rich, low-glycemic diets and ketogenic approaches improve metabolic and reproductive health, particularly in PCOS management. Specific micronutrients demonstrate protective effects: phosphorus and vitamin B12 reduce cervical and endometrial cancer risk, while increased fruit, vegetable, and vitamin D intake prevents uterine fibroids. The review further establishes that maternal dietary patterns regulate the gut microbiome, which cascades into altered metabolite profiles affecting neonatal epigenetic programming, highlighting nutrition's systemic impact across reproductive health trajectories.

title: Endometriosis and Ovarian Cancer: Insights from NHANES and Mendelian Randomization Analysis
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis, the growth of endometrial-like tissue outside the uterus affecting approximately 10% of reproductive-age women, causes chronic pain, dysmenorrhea, pelvic discomfort, inflammation, and infertility through mechanisms involving genetic, environmental, hormonal, and immune factors. This study using NHANES data and Mendelian randomization analysis demonstrates a substantial association between endometriosis and ovarian cancer, with women with endometriosis showing an 11.4-fold increased risk; notably, approximately 20% of ovarian and deep endometriosis lesions harbor somatic mutations associated with cancer development, particularly in ovarian and deep infiltrating endometriosis phenotypes. Beyond ovarian cancer, endometriosis is associated with elevated risks for thyroid cancer and overall cancer burden (OR=1.80), though with reduced cervical cancer risk and minimal breast cancer risk. Importantly, endometriosis patients demonstrated significantly lower overall survival (74.65%) compared to unaffected women, an effect driven primarily by increased non-cancer mortality rather than cancer-related deaths. Eight specific single nucleotide polymorphisms (rs851983, rs13211170, rs4735131, rs1537377, rs481772, rs10917130, rs9312658, rs9383568) were identified as genetic variants linking endometriosis susceptibility to ovarian cancer risk. Treatment options including oral contraceptives, progestins, GnRH agonists, and in severe cases surgical excision or hysterectomy are available, though endometriosis remains underdiagnosed and shows demographic disparities, with higher prevalence in non-Hispanic White women.

title: Clinical Factors Affecting the Quality of Life of Women With Endometriosis
summary: Endometriosis significantly impacts women's quality of life through multiple interconnected mechanisms spanning pain, fertility, and psychological wellbeing. The condition causes chronic pelvic pain manifestations including painful menstruation, dyspareunia, and painful urination/defecation, while simultaneously compromising fertility through fallopian tube fibrosis, pelvic adhesions, and tissue inflammation. Beyond physical symptoms, endometriosis triggers substantial psychological consequences including anxiety, depression, and social isolation, alongside strain on intimate relationships and reduced sexual desire. Quality of life deterioration is particularly pronounced across multiple domains: dyspareunia most strongly impairs sexual function, painful periods and intercourse diminish emotional wellbeing and work capacity, while infertility and surgical treatment negatively affect sense of control and self-image. Potential biomarkers including BDNF, BCL-6, and FUT4 may facilitate non-invasive diagnosis, though comprehensive management requires multimodal approaches combining NSAIDs, hormonal therapies, surgical intervention when necessary, and psychological support including cognitive behavioral therapy and relaxation techniques to address both somatic and psychological dimensions of the disease.

title: Diagnostic Potential of Serum Circulating miRNAs for Endometriosis in Patients with Chronic Pelvic Pain
summary: Endometriosis is a chronic gynecological condition characterized by ectopic endometrial-like tissue that causes inflammation, pain, infertility, and multiple pelvic symptoms including dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and dysuria. This pilot study investigated whether serum circulating microRNA profiling combined with machine learning algorithms could serve as a non-invasive diagnostic tool to differentiate endometriosis from other causes of chronic pelvic pain. Feature selection reduced an initial dataset of 4,285 miRNAs to 20 informative candidates, with logistic regression and random forest achieving the highest diagnostic performance (AUC 0.84 and 0.81, respectively), while support vector machine demonstrated superior precision (0.80) and accuracy (0.71). Key biomarker miRNAs including hsa-miR-21-5p, hsa-miR-224-5p, and hsa-miR-199a-5p were elevated in endometriosis patients and regulate pathways involved in cell proliferation, angiogenesis, and apoptosis inhibition. Implementation of serum miRNA profiling could significantly reduce diagnostic delays and unnecessary repeated surgeries by providing an accurate, non-invasive alternative to laparoscopy, which currently remains the gold standard for peritoneal endometriosis diagnosis.

title: The Role of Plant-Based Diets and Personalized Nutrition in Endometriosis Management: A Review
summary: Endometriosis is a chronic, estrogen-dependent inflammatory condition characterized by systemic inflammation and associated with multiple comorbidities including autoimmune diseases, metabolic disorders, cardiovascular disease, gastrointestinal dysfunction, and increased cancer risk, while causing debilitating symptoms such as chronic pelvic pain, heavy menstrual bleeding, infertility, and psychological manifestations including anxiety and depression. The pathophysiology involves dysregulated immune cells (neutrophils, macrophages, natural killer cells, and dendritic cells) that promote angiogenesis and perpetuate inflammation. Dietary modifications, particularly plant-based diets rich in antioxidants, phytochemicals, dietary fiber, vitamins C and E, and calcium and vitamin D, can reduce systemic inflammation and estrogen levels, thereby alleviating pain and other symptoms. Conversely, consumption of red meat, saturated fats, and trans fats elevates estradiol and estrone sulfate levels while stimulating pro-inflammatory molecules, thereby increasing endometriosis risk and severity. Personalized dietary interventions, including gluten and dairy reduction, low-FODMAP approaches for patients with concurrent irritable bowel syndrome, and incorporation of anti-inflammatory compounds such as curcumin and ginger, have demonstrated clinical benefits including reduced pelvic pain, improved gastrointestinal symptoms, and enhanced mental health and quality of life outcomes.

title: Endometriosis and Mortality Risk in US Women: Findings From NHANES 1999–2006
summary:
# Summary

This study examined mortality risk in US women with endometriosis using National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey data from 1999–2006. Endometriosis, an estrogen-dependent chronic inflammatory condition affecting the ovaries, fallopian tubes, and peritoneum, is characterized by chronic pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and infertility in up to 50% of affected women. While overall endometriosis showed non-significant associations with all-cause, cancer, and cardiovascular disease mortality, the analysis revealed a significant elevated cardiovascular mortality risk (HR 4.37) specifically in women diagnosed at age ≤30 years. The proposed mechanism underlying this increased mortality risk involves endometriosis-induced alterations in hepatic and adipose tissue metabolism that drive systemic inflammation, oxidative stress, endothelial dysfunction, and pro-atherogenic lipid profiles—all established cardiovascular disease drivers. Additionally, early surgical interventions such as bilateral oophorectomy, which reduce ovarian sex steroid production and are associated with increased cancer and cardiovascular risks, combined with endometriosis-related early natural menopause, may further contribute to premature mortality in affected women.

title: Knowledge, attitudes, and practices of patients with endometriosis regarding endometriosis surgery and postoperative care in Liaoning Province, China: a cross-sectional analysis
summary: Endometriosis is a chronic, estrogen-dependent gynecological disorder characterized by adhesions and fibrosis that lead to chronic pain, bowel and urinary dysfunction, and heightened risks of depression and anxiety. While surgical intervention remains the most effective treatment option, many patients experience pain recurrence and require reoperation, particularly those with deep infiltrating disease. This cross-sectional study in Liaoning Province, China demonstrates that patient knowledge, attitudes, and practices regarding endometriosis surgery and postoperative care are significantly interconnected, with knowledge positively predicting both attitude formation and compliance with postoperative care protocols. Multivariate analysis revealed that patient knowledge was the strongest predictor of postoperative practice compliance, with targeted health education increasing adherence by approximately 50 percent. Additionally, postoperative adherence was significantly influenced by socioeconomic status and cultural beliefs alongside knowledge level. These findings highlight that enhancing patient education and awareness can substantially improve postoperative outcomes and reduce complications in endometriosis management.

title: Association Between Dysmenorrhea and Endometrial Cancer: A Mendelian Randomization Study
summary: This Mendelian randomization study provides novel causal evidence for an inverse association between dysmenorrhea and endometrial cancer risk, with dysmenorrhea associated with an approximately 12–13% reduction in cancer incidence. The protective mechanism appears to involve dysmenorrhea-associated inflammatory responses and hormonal fluctuations, including elevated prostaglandins, tumor necrosis factor-alpha, and interleukin-6, which may activate adaptive immune responses and enhance endometrial resistance to malignant transformation. Additionally, the frequent menstrual shedding and endometrial regeneration characteristic of dysmenorrhea may remove senescent cells and prevent the unopposed estrogen exposure that typically drives endometrial carcinogenesis. Notably, pain severity, analgesic use, and endometriosis itself showed no significant association with endometrial cancer risk, suggesting that the protective effect of dysmenorrhea is independent of pain intensity or NSAID treatment. The findings challenge the assumption that dysmenorrhea uniformly increases cancer risk and suggest that cyclical hormonal fluctuations and prostaglandin-induced endometrial turnover may create a more balanced endocrine environment that reduces carcinogenic potential. While endometrial cancer remains influenced by multiple genetic, hormonal, and environmental factors including obesity, diabetes, and hypertension, this study identifies dysmenorrhea as a potentially protective menstrual phenotype.

title: Patient experience surveys for endometriosis care: A scoping review
summary: This scoping review examines patient experience surveys in endometriosis care, revealing that endometriosis significantly impacts multiple domains of patient wellbeing. The condition is strongly associated with chronic pain, heavy periods, and nausea, which collectively drive substantial effects on both mental and physical health through pathways involving infertility, stress, and social exclusion. Beyond physical symptoms, stigma and perceptions of uncaring medical professionals substantially diminish patient satisfaction with care, while diagnostic delays and limited access to services worsen overall outcomes. The COVID-19 pandemic particularly exacerbated these challenges by creating additional delays in necessary care and heightening pandemic-related stress, further compromising mental and physical health. The review identifies that comprehensive patient-centered care requires interdisciplinary approaches addressing all dimensions of wellbeing, with the ENDOCARE Questionnaire emerging as the only survey adequately covering all eight domains of person-centered care principles, including effective treatment by trusted professionals.

title: Factors contributing to the delayed diagnosis of endometriosis—a systematic review and meta-analysis
summary:
# Summary

This systematic review and meta-analysis identifies multiple interconnected factors that delay endometriosis diagnosis, which itself causes dysmenorrhea, chronic pelvic pain, dyspareunia, and infertility. Patient-related factors—including symptom normalization driven by maternal attitudes, menstrual stigma, and cultural taboos around menstruation—substantially contribute to diagnostic delays averaging over 14 years. Provider-related factors are equally significant, with general practitioners frequently misdiagnosing or dismissing endometriosis symptoms as irritable bowel syndrome or stress-related conditions, while acting as gatekeepers that restrict timely access to obstetrician-gynecologists who significantly reduce diagnostic delay. System-level barriers such as inadequate referral pathways, poor public awareness, geographic disparities, and reliance on invasive diagnostic procedures like laparoscopy further compound delays. The consequences of delayed diagnosis are severe, including worsening symptoms, reduced quality of life, increased psychological burden including frustration and stress, greater infertility risk particularly in rectovaginal disease, and higher healthcare costs. Timely diagnosis enables effective interventions including robotic-assisted surgery, which effectively reduces dysmenorrhea and chronic pelvic pain with low complication rates, underscoring the critical need for structural healthcare improvements and enhanced patient education.

title: The Association of Dietary and Psychological Variables with Infertility
summary: This study identified significant associations between psychological factors and infertility in women. Moderate-to-severe anxiety and high perceived stress emerged as strong independent predictors of infertility, with anxiety increasing infertility odds by approximately twofold and perceived stress by 1.86-fold. Notably, the belief of being overweight showed an even stronger association with infertility, increasing odds by 2.59-fold, suggesting that body image perception may substantially impact fertility outcomes. Interestingly, depression did not demonstrate a significant association with infertility in multivariate analysis, distinguishing it from anxiety and stress as psychological risk factors. These findings underscore the interconnected relationship between psychological well-being, body perception, and reproductive health, highlighting the importance of addressing anxiety and stress management in infertility interventions.

title: Single-Cell Profiling Reveals Altered Endometrial Cellular Features Across the Menstrual Cycle in Endometriosis Patients
summary: This single-cell profiling study reveals that endometriosis fundamentally disrupts endometrial cellular homeostasis through multiple interconnected mechanisms. The research identifies previously uncharacterized subpopulations of endometrial fibroblasts and epithelial cells that undergo epithelial-mesenchymal transition, a process that contributes to the condition's pathophysiology. Comparative gene expression analysis implicates oxidative stress, aberrant cell migration, and dysregulated apoptosis as key molecular drivers associated with endometriosis, while the disease also causes widespread inflammation and disrupts normal immune cell communication networks. These cellular alterations occur across menstrual cycle phases in endometriosis patients, indicating sustained dysregulation rather than phase-specific changes. The altered eutopic endometrial homeostasis documented in this study has important implications for the clinical manifestations of endometriosis, particularly the associated pain and infertility that affect millions of women globally. Understanding these cellular-level changes provides potential targets for therapeutic intervention in this estrogen-dependent chronic condition.

title: Comparative analysis of hysterosalpingography and hysterosalpingo-contrast sonography for assessing tubal patency in women with endometriosis-related infertility: a propensity score-matched study
summary:
# Summary

This propensity score-matched comparative study evaluated hysterosalpingography (HSG) and hysterosalpingo-contrast sonography (HyCoSy) for assessing tubal patency in women with endometriosis-related infertility. Women receiving HSG with water-based contrast demonstrated significantly higher clinical pregnancy rates compared to those receiving HyCoSy, likely due to the iodine-based contrast medium's multi-faceted mechanisms: mechanical flushing of tubal debris, anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties, and immunomodulatory effects on endometrial dendritic cells and T cell populations. Iodine involvement in thyroid hormone synthesis and ovulation promotion, combined with enhanced embryo implantation and endometrial restoration, provides additional biological rationale for HSG's superior fertility outcomes in this population. While HyCoSy offers the advantage of avoiding radiation exposure and has shown potential to improve spontaneous pregnancy rates, the study suggests HSG with water-based contrast is more effective for improving fertility in women with endometriosis, whose condition is characterized by elevated inflammation and an immunosuppressive microenvironment.

title: Feasibility, acceptability, and exploratory outcomes of a virtual cognitive behavioural therapy‐based group intervention for persistent fatigue in endometriosis: The Managing Fatigue in Endometriosis (MEND) programme
summary: Endometriosis causes multiple debilitating symptoms including chronic pelvic pain, heavy menstrual bleeding, infertility, and persistent fatigue—a symptom frequently overlooked in clinical practice despite being associated with depression, anxiety, and reduced quality of life. Fatigue in endometriosis arises from complex physiological pathways involving inflammation, immune dysfunction, and disease-related tissue damage, combined with psychological and behavioral factors. The Managing Fatigue in Endometriosis (MEND) programme is a six-session virtual cognitive behavioral therapy intervention designed to target these perpetuating factors by addressing unhelpful thought patterns, sleep disruption, and activity avoidance. The study demonstrated that MEND was feasible and acceptable to participants, with 57% completing the intervention, and participants particularly valued sessions on sleep and pacing strategies for understanding their fatigue. While fatigue outcomes showed variable improvements (18–55% of participants improved, with some showing no change or deterioration), qualitative feedback indicated participants found the group format validating and helpful for reducing isolation. These findings suggest cognitive behavioral therapy delivered virtually has promise for managing endometriosis-related fatigue, though larger-scale research is needed to establish efficacy and optimize retention.

title: Identification and Validation of Novel Combinatorial Genetic Risk Factors for Endometriosis across Multiple UK and US Patient Cohorts
summary: This study identified and validated novel combinatorial genetic risk factors for endometriosis through analysis of large UK and US patient cohorts. The research discovered 1,709 disease signatures composed of 2-5 single nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs) associated with endometriosis, including 75 previously unidentified genes and 9 novel genes linked to autophagy and macrophage biology. Beyond individual genetic variants, the combinatorial analysis revealed that pathways enriched in endometriosis disease signatures include cell adhesion, proliferation, migration, cytoskeleton remodeling, angiogenesis, fibrosis, and neuropathic pain mechanisms. The study confirmed that endometriosis is a multifactorial disease causing severe chronic pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, and infertility in up to 30-50% of affected women, while frequently co-occurring with migraine, irritable bowel syndrome, interstitial cystitis, and fibromyalgia. Notably, 35 of 42 previously identified meta-GWAS loci were successfully validated in these independent cohorts, demonstrating the reproducibility of the genetic signatures. These findings suggest that examining combinations of genetic variants rather than isolated SNPs provides improved understanding of endometriosis pathogenesis and may inform future therapeutic strategies targeting the identified biological pathways.

title: Infertility burden among women firefighters: a cross-sectional exploratory analysis
summary: This cross-sectional study examined infertility burden among female firefighters and identified occupational factors potentially contributing to reproductive health challenges in this population. The research found that firefighting occupation itself was significantly associated with infertility, suggesting that biological and environmental exposures inherent to firefighting work may compromise fertility. Additionally, longer duration of employment as a firefighter showed a modest, though not statistically significant, positive association with infertility risk, indicating that cumulative occupational exposure may play a role in reproductive dysfunction. The study addresses a previously understudied intersection of occupational health and reproductive medicine, highlighting that female firefighters face unique occupational risk factors that warrant further investigation into the underlying mechanisms linking firefighting exposures to infertility. These findings suggest that occupational and environmental factors in firefighting warrant consideration alongside other known contributors to infertility, including conditions like endometriosis and associated comorbidities such as pain and mental health impacts.

title: “It’s like your body is fighting against you”: a cross-sectional qualitative interview study of quality of life in U.S. Black women diagnosed with endometriosis
summary: This qualitative study examines how endometriosis affects quality of life in Black women, revealing a constellation of debilitating physical symptoms including chronic pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, heavy menstrual bleeding, pain during intercourse, and gastrointestinal complications such as constipation and urogenital pain. Beyond physical manifestations, endometriosis is strongly associated with significant psychological distress—including anxiety, depression, fear, hopelessness, and loneliness—and substantially impairs intimate relationships, social functioning, and self-perception. The research identifies structural barriers to care, notably that provider bias and dismissal of Black patients' symptoms contribute to systemic underdiagnosis of endometriosis in this population, with delayed diagnosis prolonging suffering and potentially resulting in preventable complications such as removal of reproductive organs. While standard treatments including laparoscopy, hormonal birth control, and pain management can reduce symptoms, and diagnosis itself provides relief and validation, Black women often must rely on burdensome self-advocacy to access adequate care within a healthcare system shaped by structural racism. The findings underscore that endometriosis management and quality of life outcomes for Black women are constrained by both disease severity and systemic inequities, highlighting critical gaps in clinical recognition and research representation.

title: Prevalence and risk factors of heavy menstrual bleeding in Africa: a narrative review
summary: Heavy menstrual bleeding (HMB) represents a significant reproductive health burden in Africa with multifactorial etiology and profound clinical consequences. Structural uterine pathologies, particularly uterine fibroids which are disproportionately prevalent in African women, along with adenomyosis, endometrial polyps, and endometriosis, are major causes of HMB, while systemic conditions including HIV infection, malaria, anemia, diabetes, and hypertension substantially increase disease risk. Nutritional deficiencies in iron, folate, and vitamin D further exacerbate HMB prevalence, and antiretroviral therapy for HIV can independently trigger menstrual irregularities through immunological and hormonal mechanisms. The physical, emotional, and social burden of HMB is substantial, leading to iron deficiency anemia, fatigue, weakness, pelvic pain, anxiety, depression, and stress, with women in their 30s and 40s and those with higher parity facing elevated risk. Critically, underlying causes of HMB such as fibroids, endometriosis, and polycystic ovary syndrome are independently associated with infertility and pregnancy complications, while NSAIDs provide moderate therapeutic benefit. Addressing HMB in African women requires comprehensive approaches targeting both the underlying pathological causes and the associated nutritional and systemic health deficiencies.

title: Six to seven times less likely to be physically active: Low self-determination and basic psychological needs support in women with endometriosis—A cluster analysis
summary: This study identifies critical psychological barriers to physical activity in women with endometriosis, a multifaceted condition characterized by dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, chronic fatigue, and infertility. Cluster analysis revealed that women with endometriosis fall into distinct motivational profiles: those with more controlled behavior patterns or thwarted basic psychological needs (autonomy, competence, relatedness) were six to seven times less likely to engage in physical activity compared to women with autonomous motivation. Self-determined motivation—characterized by intrinsic regulation and sense of choice—was strongly associated with improved exercise behaviors and better adaptive outcomes, whereas controlled motivation and amotivation predicted unhealthy behavioral disengagement. The study demonstrates that fulfilling basic psychological needs and promoting autonomous forms of motivation may improve physical activity engagement, offering a psychological pathway to address the symptom burden of endometriosis. These findings align with broader patterns observed in other chronic conditions such as breast cancer and chronic pain, suggesting that motivational support targeting autonomy, competence, and social connectedness could enhance conservative treatment adherence in endometriosis management.

title: Clinical Investigation of Recurrence, Oncological, and Obstetrical Outcomes in Patients with Ovarian Atypical Endometriosis
summary:
# Summary

This clinical investigation examines outcomes in patients with atypical endometriosis, a precancerous form of endometriosis characterized by cytologic atypia and architectural hyperplasia. Atypical endometriosis carries significantly higher recurrence rates than typical endometriosis and is strongly associated with malignant transformation to endometriosis-associated ovarian cancer, with molecular alterations including ARID1A/BAF250a, PIK3CA, CTNNB1, and PTEN mutations implicated in cancer progression. While cyst enucleation is the primary surgical treatment, prior ovarian endometrioma surgery emerges as the sole independent predictor of recurrence. Notably, in vitro fertilization does not increase endometriosis recurrence or cancer risk in atypical endometriosis patients, instead reducing infertility-related symptoms and pain, making it a safe reproductive option. The underlying mechanism linking endometriosis to malignancy involves cyclic hemorrhage within cysts triggering inflammation and DNA damage. Overall, these findings suggest that while atypical endometriosis poses elevated cancer risk, surgical treatment combined with assisted reproductive technology can effectively manage both infertility and recurrence concerns in affected patients.

title: Global, regional, and national burdens of endometriosis from 1990 to 2021: a trend analysis
summary:
# Summary

This global burden analysis (1990–2021) demonstrates that endometriosis affects approximately 5–10% of women of reproductive age, with peak incidence in those aged 20–29 years, reflecting its estrogen-dependent nature. The condition causes substantial clinical and psychological morbidity through pelvic pain, infertility, depression, and fatigue, reducing work productivity and quality of life—particularly affecting 25–50% of infertile women and 71–87% of those with chronic pelvic pain. Global disease burden shows striking regional variation, with highest incidence and disability-adjusted life years in low sociodemographic index regions (particularly South Asia, East Asia, and Niger) and lowest rates in high-income North America, a pattern positively correlated with the Human Development Index. From 1990 to 2021, improved diagnostic capabilities, heightened awareness, and enhanced treatment options (medication, hormone therapy, minimally invasive surgery) reduced incidence and death rates in developed regions, while low-income countries experienced increased burdens due to rapid population growth and proportionally younger female populations. Early diagnosis and hormone management strategies targeting ovarian activity suppression and steroid receptors offer pathways to prevent disease progression, though a persistent 7–9 year diagnostic delay remains a clinical challenge requiring urgent intervention.

title: First birth and total fertility rate in women with surgically verified endometriosis – A nationwide register study of 18 320 women across reproductive life course
summary:
# Summary

This nationwide register study of 18,320 women with surgically verified endometriosis examined fertility outcomes across the reproductive lifespan and found that endometriosis significantly compromises fertility compared to reference women, with only 68.2% of women with endometriosis giving birth versus 80.7% in the general population. The effect of endometriosis on fertility varied substantially by subtype: women with peritoneal and deep endometriosis had higher first birth rates (73.1% and 71.3%, respectively), while those with ovarian or combined endometriosis had substantially lower rates, likely reflecting delayed diagnosis since ovarian endometriosis is often asymptomatic. Women with endometriosis had a four-fold higher rate of registered infertility and used IVF treatments more than five times more frequently than controls, yet this increased medical intervention did not fully compensate for the fertility deficit. The total fertility rate was significantly lower in women with endometriosis (1.33 children) compared to reference women (1.89 children), demonstrating that endometriosis reduces overall completed fertility. These findings underscore that while timely diagnosis and appropriate treatment may improve outcomes, the fertility compromise associated with endometriosis persists across different presentation subtypes, with the burden greatest for ovarian and combined disease presentations.

title: High-Resolution Spatial Transcriptomics Reveals Fibroblast and Neuroimmune Microenvironments in Endometriosis Lesions
summary: Using high-resolution spatial transcriptomics, this study reveals the complex microenvironmental architecture of endometriosis lesions by mapping the precise cellular organization and interactions within peritoneal lesions containing ectopic endometrium-like tissue. The researchers identified consistent spatial features across lesion types, including dense immune cell infiltration dominated by macrophages, fibroblast-rich compartments surrounding epithelial glands, and previously uncharacterized sensory neuronal subtypes positioned in proximity to immune cells. Endometriosis causes chronic pain and inflammation through this integrated neuroimmune microenvironment, where the spatial relationship between sensory neurons and immune cell populations likely contributes to pain sensitization. The characterization of fibroblast specialization and immune cell composition provides mechanistic insight into how local tissue remodeling and neuroinflammation drive the symptomatic pathology of endometriosis. These findings establish a cellular and spatial framework for understanding how endometriosis simultaneously affects pain perception, immune function, and tissue homeostasis in peritoneal lesions.

title: Exploring positive and negative body image and health-related quality of life in women with endometriosis: a latent profile analysis
summary:
# Summary

This study examined how body image relates to health-related quality of life in women with endometriosis using latent profile analysis. Endometriosis causes multiple debilitating symptoms—including chronic pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, dyschezia, and menstrual irregularities—alongside anxiety, depression, and poor body image, all contributing to substantially diminished quality of life. The research identified distinct body image profiles, revealing that women with strong body appreciation and low body dissatisfaction experienced better overall quality of life and fewer endometriosis symptoms, whereas those with low body appreciation and high body dissatisfaction reported the poorest outcomes, including elevated symptom burden, emotional distress, and reduced social support. Positive body image functioned as a protective factor, buffering against endometriosis's adverse impacts by enhancing emotional resilience, perceived social support, and physical functioning, while negative body image intensified symptom perception and impaired both physical and psychological health. The findings suggest that body image configurations are critical psychosocial factors in endometriosis management, with interventions targeting body appreciation potentially offering meaningful improvements in quality of life alongside standard medical treatments.

title: Adverse childhood experiences and the risk of endometriosis—a nationwide cohort study
summary: This nationwide cohort study demonstrates a robust association between adverse childhood experiences (ACEs) and endometriosis risk, with exposure to violence showing the strongest association and cumulative adversities producing dose-dependent effects—women with five or more ACEs exhibited a 60% increased risk. Multiple specific ACEs linked to endometriosis include parental substance abuse, psychiatric disorders, intellectual disability, parental separation, residential instability, child welfare intervention, and exposure to violence, though familial death showed no significant association. The proposed mechanisms connecting ACEs to endometriosis involve immune dysregulation, chronic systemic inflammation, altered hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis function, and increased pain sensitization, which together create conditions permitting endometrial lesion implantation and development. Since endometriosis commonly presents with dysmenorrhea, infertility, and chronic pain—conditions that are themselves associated with ACEs and psychiatric comorbidities—the authors recommend comprehensive gynecological evaluation in individuals with childhood trauma histories and emphasize multidisciplinary treatment approaches incorporating psychological interventions.

title: Effect of education on endometriosis and mediating effects: a mendelian randomization study
summary: This Mendelian randomization study demonstrates a protective causal effect of educational attainment on endometriosis risk, with each 4.2-year increase in education reducing endometriosis odds by approximately 0.25%. The protective effect of education operates substantially through mental health and sleep pathways: depression mediates 53% of the education-endometriosis association, while insomnia and anxiety each mediate approximately 30-32%, and the combined effect of all three psychological factors accounts for 64% of the protective association. Education reduces the risk of endometriosis partly by lowering rates of depression, anxiety, and insomnia—psychiatric and sleep disorders that independently increase endometriosis susceptibility. Notably, insomnia emerges as a significant risk factor for endometriosis itself, while endometriosis causes considerable morbidity including chronic pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, and infertility, which in turn generate psychological distress. These findings suggest that the education-endometriosis relationship involves a bidirectional psychosomatic pathway where higher educational attainment protects against the disease by reducing psychological burden and sleep disturbances.

title: Attribute preferences associated with gonadotropin-releasing hormone agonists/antagonists among women with endometriosis in the United States
summary: Endometriosis affects women through multiple debilitating symptoms including dysmenorrhea, non-menstrual pelvic pain, dyspareunia, and systemic manifestations such as fatigue, bowel symptoms, and urinary difficulties, with high prevalence of comorbid depression and anxiety substantially impairing quality of life and work productivity. Current pharmacologic treatments include hormonal contraceptives, progestins, danazol, and GnRH agonists/antagonists, which treat the disease directly, while NSAIDs and opioids provide symptomatic pain relief only. Among women selecting GnRH agonist/antagonist therapy, relief of non-menstrual pelvic pain emerged as the most influential treatment attribute, followed by improvements in dyspareunia and dysmenorrhea. Beyond pain management, women prioritize non-pain attributes including short treatment onset, long-term safety, and fatigue reduction when choosing endometriosis medications, while lesser-valued attributes include dosage flexibility, sleep disruption avoidance, and need for additional contraceptives. The shift from injectable to oral GnRH agonist/antagonist formulations, along with reduced risk of hot flashes and bone fractures, represents meaningful clinical advances that align with patient preferences for convenient, safer treatment options.

title: Designing and validating a health promotion program for women with endometriosis: a mixed-methods study protocol
summary: Endometriosis is a chronic, estrogen-dependent gynecological condition characterized by ectopic endometrial tissue implantation in pelvic sites including the ovaries, uterosacral ligaments, and bowel, which triggers a chronic inflammatory reaction. Women with endometriosis experience a multifaceted symptom burden encompassing reproductive complications such as infertility and painful intercourse, alongside gastrointestinal symptoms including bloating, diarrhea, constipation, and painful defecation, as well as periodic and non-periodic abdominal pain. Beyond physical manifestations, endometriosis significantly impacts psychological well-being, sexual and marital relationships, and occupational functioning, reflecting its pervasive effect on overall quality of life. This mixed-methods protocol proposes designing and validating a health promotion intervention based on evidence that health-promoting lifestyles serve as fundamental disease prevention strategies and effectively improve both health outcomes and quality of life in chronic disease populations. The study employs validated assessment tools including the Health-Promoting Lifestyle Profile (HPLP-II) and the Endometriosis Impact Questionnaire (EIQ) to comprehensively evaluate intervention efficacy across physical, psychological, and social domains affected by endometriosis.

title: Disease burden of infertility in five East Asian countries from 1990 to 2021 and prediction for 2050: An analysis of the Global Burden of Disease study 2021
summary:
# Summary

This Global Burden of Disease analysis examined infertility prevalence across five East Asian countries from 1990 to 2021, revealing that endometriosis, along with ovulation dysfunction, tubal blockage, and uterine abnormalities, represents a major cause of female infertility, which is highest in East Asia and developing countries within the region. Male infertility, accounting for 30-50% of infertility cases, is commonly associated with poor semen quality and sexual dysfunction, with rates increasing in economically developing areas like Mongolia. Beyond biological mechanisms, the study found that infertility creates substantial psychological burden, causing anxiety and emotional distress, while workplace stress and occupational hazards in high-demand professions such as surgery contribute to increased miscarriage and infertility rates. The research demonstrates that infertility's impact extends beyond individual physical and mental health to societal consequences, including population decline and increased pressure on social systems, particularly in cultures where fertility is highly emphasized. These findings underscore the need for comprehensive approaches addressing both the medical and psychosocial dimensions of infertility in East Asian populations.

title: Global, regional, and national burden of endometriosis among women of childbearing age from 1990 to 2021: a cross-sectional analysis from the 2021 global burden of disease study
summary:
# Summary

This Global Burden of Disease study presents a comprehensive epidemiological analysis of endometriosis among women of childbearing age from 1990 to 2021, revealing significant clinical associations and geographic disparities. Endometriosis exhibits strong associations with reproductive complications including infertility (affecting approximately 50% of infertile women), pregnancy loss, and gestational complications, alongside substantial pain symptoms such as dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and pelvic pain. The condition also demonstrates elevated risks for depression, anxiety, autoimmune diseases, migraines, and cancers, reflecting its systemic inflammatory and estrogen-dependent pathophysiology characterized by ectopic endometrial tissue invasion, implantation, and recurrence. Despite these burdens, global age-standardized prevalence and incidence rates declined from 1990 to 2021, with projections indicating further decreases by 2040, driven primarily by population growth and epidemiologic shifts. Notably, low sociodemographic index regions and specific geographic areas including Oceania, Eastern Europe, and African nations (Niger, Chad) bear disproportionately higher disease burdens, while diagnostic delays remain a critical challenge stemming from insidious disease onset, inadequate detection, and reliance on invasive laparoscopic surgery as the diagnostic gold standard.

title: The Voice of Endo: Leveraging Speech for an Intelligent System That Can Forecast Illness Flare-ups
journal: Proceedings of the SIGCHI conference on human factors in computing systems. CHI Conference
summary: Endometriosis is a chronic inflammatory disease affecting approximately 10% of people assigned female sex at birth, characterized by chronic pelvic pain, subfertility, fatigue, and bloating, with symptoms manifesting either cyclically or unpredictably as flare-ups that disrupt daily routines. The heterogeneous and unpredictable nature of endometriosis, combined with the lack of a diagnostic biomarker to monitor disease progression, creates significant challenges for patient management and symptom forecasting. This study explores voice analysis as a novel approach to predict endometriosis flare-ups, leveraging emerging evidence that vocal acoustic and prosodic features can detect physiological well-being and have been successfully associated with other chronic conditions including depression, Parkinson's disease, and autism spectrum disorder. Voice recordings offer additional benefits by validating patients' illness experiences and providing emotional support through the act of regular monitoring, while extracted auditory and contextual features from speech may serve as surrogate markers of inflammation. The research builds on evidence from diabetes and other chronic conditions demonstrating that personalized forecasts improve health-related decision-making and patient outcomes. This approach represents a promising strategy to help endometriosis patients anticipate flare-ups and take preemptive health actions despite the condition's inherent unpredictability.

title: A machine learning approach towards endometriosis screening using infrared spectra of urine
summary: Endometriosis is a chronic gynaecological disorder that causes multiple debilitating symptoms including chronic pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, infertility, anxiety, and depression, with diagnostic delays significantly worsening quality of life. This study proposes a non-invasive machine learning approach using Attenuated Total Reflection Fourier Transform Infrared (ATR-FTIR) spectroscopy to detect distinctive patterns in urinary lipids, proteins, and carbohydrates that differ between endometriosis patients and controls. The sensitivity-tuned algorithm achieved 93% sensitivity with 57% specificity, while the specificity-tuned version reached 93% specificity, and an MC-GA-LDA algorithm achieved 73% sensitivity and 79% specificity, substantially reducing unnecessary MRI referrals compared to current diagnostic methods. The urine-based machine learning test offers a rapid, non-invasive alternative to invasive laparoscopy and costly imaging techniques, with potential to accelerate clinical decision-making and significantly reduce diagnostic delays and healthcare costs. This approach demonstrates clinical promise as a screening tool that could optimize healthcare resource allocation while improving timely endometriosis diagnosis.

title: Educational and occupational outcomes during early adulthood following surgically verified endometriosis at a young age—A register‐based study
summary:
# Summary

This register-based study demonstrates that surgically verified endometriosis diagnosed at a young age is associated with substantial reductions in both educational and occupational attainment in early adulthood. Beyond the well-documented physical symptoms—pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and infertility—endometriosis carries significant psychosocial burdens, with high rates of comorbid depression and anxiety that further compound adverse outcomes. Women with endometriosis are less likely to complete tertiary education and more frequently occupy lower-level occupational positions, with particularly poor outcomes associated with painful endometriosis subtypes. However, the data reveal important phenotypic variation: women with ovarian-only endometriosis, typically associated with less pain, show more favorable educational and occupational trajectories compared to those with deep endometriosis. Notably, pain and preexisting depression or anxiety emerge as key mediating factors suppressing educational achievement and occupational advancement, suggesting that symptom management and mental health support are critical intervention targets to improve life outcomes in this population.

title: The Diversity of Developmental Age Gynecology—Selected Issues
summary: This review examines the spectrum of gynecological conditions affecting adolescents, with pelvic pain syndrome representing the most prevalent diagnosis at 77.8% of cases, though its incidence has significantly decreased in recent years while androgenization syndromes have risen correspondingly.
