This study demonstrates that endometriosis, particularly moderate to severe stages and the combined phenotype of ovarian endometrioma with deep infiltrating lesions, is significantly associated with elevated triglyceride levels and hypertriglyceridemia. Women with the most severe endometriosis phenotype exhibit a 3.59-fold increased prevalence of hypertriglyceridemia, with levels peaking during the follicular phase when estrogen concentrations are highest. The mechanism underlying this association appears to involve sphingomyelin accumulation in endometriotic tissues, which disrupts lipid metabolism and increases LDL uptake, potentially amplified by elevated estrogen levels. Since hypertriglyceridemia independently raises the risk for cardiovascular disease, stroke, and pregnancy-related complications including preeclampsia and gestational diabetes, the heightened triglyceride levels in women with severe endometriosis may explain their increased cardiovascular and metabolic morbidity. These findings underscore the importance of monitoring lipid profiles in endometriosis patients, particularly those with advanced disease stages, to mitigate long-term health consequences.

title: Age-dependent variations in clinical features and operative time for endometriotic cysts
summary: Endometriosis is an estrogen-dependent gynecological condition that commonly causes dysmenorrhea, chronic pelvic pain, dyspareunia, and infertility through inflammatory responses and tissue adhesions, with endometriotic cysts specifically impairing ovarian function and distorting pelvic anatomy. This study demonstrates striking age-dependent variations in disease presentation and surgical management, with patients older than 30 years presenting with larger cyst volumes, higher ASRM stages (III-IV), more extensive pelvic adhesions, and significantly longer operative times exceeding 150 minutes compared to younger patients. Age greater than 30 years independently predicts prolonged surgical time and is associated with greater disease complexity including higher rates of recurrence, coexisting uterine disorders such as fibroids, adenomyosis, and endometrial polyps, plus elevated body mass index. Older patients exhibited substantially lower endometrial fertility index scores, indicating reduced fertility potential, while experiencing longer disease duration before diagnosis and higher rates of repeat surgeries requiring standardized preoperative hormonal therapy. Operative time complexity reflects multiple factors including cyst size, recurrence, bilateral distribution, and concomitant pathology, with older patients demonstrating reduced tissue healing capacity and increased intraoperative anatomical difficulty. These findings suggest that advancing age is fundamentally associated with greater endometriosis disease burden, complexity, and comorbidity, necessitating more extensive surgical intervention and potentially compromising reproductive outcomes.

title: Knowledge, Attitudes, and Practices Regarding Infertility Among Lebanese Women Experiencing Difficulty Conceiving
summary:
# Summary

This study examined knowledge, attitudes, and practices regarding infertility among Lebanese women experiencing conception difficulties, identifying both biological and psychosocial factors that influence reproductive outcomes. Female infertility was predominantly attributed to ovulatory disorders, endometriosis, and pelvic adhesions, with polycystic ovary syndrome accounting for 36.56% of cases, while male infertility primarily resulted from low sperm count and quality. Knowledge scores were significantly higher among healthcare providers and those sleeping ≥7 hours nightly, but lower among women of advanced reproductive age and those with obesity, suggesting that cognitive factors and sleep quality impact understanding of infertility causes. Attitudes toward infertility were notably influenced by financial circumstances, with medical insurance and higher family income associated with improved attitudes, while prolonged marriage duration, smoking history, and prior endometrial ablation worsened attitudes, likely reflecting the psychological burden of prolonged infertility. Importantly, knowledge directly improved practice scores toward infertility management, which in turn improved attitudes, demonstrating a significant cascade effect where enhanced understanding leads to better behaviors and more positive psychological responses.

title: Recommendations from the WHO guideline for the prevention, diagnosis, and treatment of infertility†
summary: The WHO guideline comprehensively addresses the prevention, diagnosis, and treatment of infertility by identifying its multifactorial etiology across both sexes. Female infertility arises from diverse causes including ovulatory dysfunction (26.1%), tubal disease (17.7%), pelvic adhesions (14.8%), hyperprolactinemia (6.7%), and endometriosis (4.8%), with additional conditions such as polycystic ovary syndrome, uterine abnormalities, and fibroids requiring targeted fertility care. Male infertility is predominantly associated with varicocele (13.1%), primary testicular failure (12%), abnormal semen parameters (9.7%), and obstructive or non-obstructive azoospermia, alongside hormonal and accessory gland abnormalities. Beyond anatomical and endocrine factors, lifestyle and environmental exposures—including tobacco use, excessive alcohol intake, cannabis use, obesity, low body weight, and workplace factors—contribute to infertility risk and require management. The guideline recommends evidence-based interventions including intracytoplasmic sperm injection for unexplained male factors, assisted reproductive techniques such as IVF and IUI, and sperm retrieval techniques for azoospermia, while also addressing fertility preservation in patients undergoing gonadotoxic therapy.

title: Endometriosis: From Genes to Global Burden
summary: This comprehensive analysis reveals endometriosis as a complex, multifactorial systemic disorder with significant molecular and clinical implications. Genomic profiling identified a distinct gene signature characterized by upregulation of key genes including FOS, DES, SIRT1, SBDS, SRF, SPN, P2RX1, TEAD3, and SLITRK3 in endometriosis patients, while KIF22, KIF25, GAS2L2, and HINT3 were significantly downregulated. FOS emerged as a particularly important regulator, controlling multicellular organismal processes and hematopoiesis while being regulated by MAPK/ERK signaling in response to oxidative stress to promote proliferation and inhibit apoptosis. Beyond genetic drivers, endometriosis associates with debilitating pain symptoms through activation of pain-related genes including NGF, BSN, MLLT10, GDAP1, and SRP14/BMF, alongside significant psychological burden with anxiety and depression representing major comorbidities accounting for substantial disability. The condition also demonstrates systemic inflammatory consequences linked to multiple comorbidities including dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, pelvic pain, infertility, migraines, and autoimmune diseases, with evidence supporting a potential malignant transformation pathway to ovarian cancer through genes like KRAS and ADAMTS19 that promote cellular proliferation and invasion.

title: Noninvasive BCL6 Preoperative Screening and Anatomic Patterns of Endometriosis in Patients with Unexplained Infertility
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that noninvasive BCL6 testing represents a valuable preoperative screening tool for endometriosis in patients with unexplained infertility. BCL6 overexpression, detected via the ReceptivaDx test with high sensitivity (93-94%), serves as a marker of endometrial inflammation and progesterone resistance, enabling earlier identification of endometriosis before diagnostic laparoscopy. Patients who received preoperative BCL6 testing exhibited significantly lower disease burden, with fewer anatomically involved sites (3.14 versus 3.93 regions) and reduced high-stage disease compared to those without testing, suggesting that noninvasive screening facilitates better surgical planning and potentially improves fertility outcomes. The integration of BCL6 testing into clinical workflows allows clinicians to triage patients for specialist referral more efficiently, reducing diagnostic delays that otherwise burden endometriosis care and delaying appropriate treatment. This approach addresses the chronic inflammation underlying endometriosis, where oxidative stress contributes to lesion proliferation, while simultaneously streamlining the diagnostic pathway to accelerate access to definitive care for infertile women harboring undetected endometriotic disease.

title: ‘I Wish I Fought for Myself More Instead of Just Letting Doctors Dismiss Me’: A Combined Qualitative Analysis of Four Cohorts of Aotearoa New Zealand Endometriosis Patients
journal: The Australian & New Zealand Journal of Obstetrics & Gynaecology
summary:
# Endometriosis Patient Experiences in Aotearoa New Zealand: A Multi-Cohort Qualitative Analysis

Endometriosis, characterized by the presence of endometrial-like cells in extra-uterine locations, significantly disrupts patients' lives across multiple domains including chronic pain, mental health, infertility, work, education, relationships, and finances. Diagnostic delays averaging 8.7–9.7 years in New Zealand are substantially perpetuated by medical practitioners' dismissal of symptoms, normalization of menstrual pain, and poor understanding of the condition, with these barriers exacerbated for Māori, Pasifika, LGBTQIA+, and overweight patients who face additional stigma and discrimination. Medical practitioners' dismissive attitudes—including characterizing patients as hysterical or drug-seeking, suggesting psychological rather than physical treatments, and ignoring consent during examinations—directly harm patient outcomes by delaying diagnosis and deterring continued healthcare engagement. Conversely, supportive practitioners who validate symptoms, provide clear explanations grounded in clinical guidelines, respect patient autonomy, and offer holistic assessments significantly enhance patient outcomes and sense of safety within the healthcare system. Socioeconomic status creates additional inequity, with private healthcare access correlating with reduced wait times and greater access to specialist services, while public system delays compound diagnostic burden. Achieving improved endometriosis care requires addressing both knowledge gaps among medical professionals and systemic barriers that disproportionately affect marginalized populations.

title: Development of an endometriosis self-care behaviors scale: a psychometric study
summary: This psychometric study developed and validated the Endometriosis Self-Care Behaviors Scale, a 17-item instrument organized into 4 factors based on Dorothea Orem's Self-Care Model to assess self-care strategies in women with endometriosis. Endometriosis causes multiple debilitating symptoms including dysmenorrhea, pelvic pain, dyspareunia, dysuria, dyschezia, and infertility, with risk factors encompassing genetic, hormonal, and environmental components alongside lifestyle factors such as unhealthy diet, alcohol consumption, trans fat and red meat consumption, and lack of regular exercise. The condition significantly impairs patients' social lives, emotional health, and quality of life, necessitating comprehensive management approaches beyond pharmacological and surgical treatments. The research emphasizes that self-care behaviors—encompassing maintenance activities like yoga and relaxation techniques, self-monitoring of symptoms, and management responses to symptom onset—substantially improve quality of life, reduce stress levels, and increase disease knowledge. Evidence from nursing intervention programs and instructional strategies demonstrates that comprehensive self-care counseling and lifestyle modifications targeting healthy diet, physical activity, and stress management effectively reduce anxiety, depression, fatigue, and pain while improving knowledge levels and self-care behaviors. The developed scale provides healthcare providers with a validated tool to assess and promote self-care strategies that support women's physical and emotional stability throughout endometriosis management.

title: Physical activity phenotypes in endometriosis using unsupervised learning via functional mixture models
summary: Endometriosis is a chronic, estrogen-mediated inflammatory condition affecting approximately 10% of women of reproductive age and characterized by pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, fatigue, infertility, and adhesion formation that substantially reduces quality of life. Using unsupervised functional mixture modeling of Fitbit-derived physical activity data, researchers identified four distinct activity phenotypes in endometriosis patients: High Active, High Moderate, Low Moderate, and Insufficiently Active, stratified by daily step counts and moderate-to-vigorous physical activity minutes. Higher-activity phenotypes were generally associated with lower pain scores, supporting evidence that regular physical activity effectively reduces endometriosis-related pain and improves quality of life, though the relationship may be complex since pain itself can impede physical activity engagement. Notably, despite demonstrating the highest activity levels, the High Active phenotype paradoxically reported the highest proportion of severe to moderate fatigue, suggesting that fatigue in endometriosis may involve mechanisms independent of activity levels or that highly active individuals may be overexerting despite symptom burden. These findings demonstrate the heterogeneity of physical activity patterns in endometriosis populations and support activity-based approaches to symptom management while highlighting the need to address fatigue through complementary interventions.

title: Neurobiological Signatures of Endometriosis: Characterizing Pain, Cognition, and Brain Morphology
summary: This study reveals a complex neurobiological phenotype in endometriosis characterized by pain, psychiatric comorbidity, and cognitive dysfunction alongside distinct brain structural changes. Women with endometriosis exhibit significantly higher levels of pain, depression, and anxiety compared to controls, with greater exposure to adverse childhood events and post-traumatic stress symptoms, suggesting a compounded psychosocial burden. The condition is associated with cognitive deficits spanning processing speed, memory, and attention, with measurable impairments in face-name association, verbal memory, and executive function. Neuroimaging demonstrates that endometriosis-related pain correlates with reduced gray matter volume in pain-processing regions including the temporal cortex, cingulate gyrus, thalamus, and insula, while simultaneously showing increased white matter microstructural changes (quantitative anisotropy) across interhemispheric, limbic, and sensorimotor pathways. These structural brain alterations appear to reflect central nervous system sensitization and reorganization rather than merely psychological consequences, as pain severity showed independent associations with morphological changes. These findings establish endometriosis as a neurobiological disorder with documented impacts on brain structure and cognitive function beyond peripheral pelvic pathology.

title: Endometriosis burden and trends among women of childbearing age from 1990 to 2021
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is a prevalent gynecological condition affecting women of childbearing age, characterized by abnormal growth of endometrial-like tissue outside the uterus, particularly in pelvic organs such as the ovaries and uterosacral ligaments. The ectopic lesions trigger localized inflammation, fibrosis, and tissue adhesions, leading to the primary clinical manifestations of chronic lower abdominal pain, dysmenorrhea, irregular menstruation, and infertility, while also contributing to anxiety, depression, and diminished quality of life. A comprehensive global burden analysis from 1990 to 2021 reveals significant disparities in endometriosis prevalence, incidence, and mortality across sociodemographic regions, with the highest prevalence rates in low sociodemographic index (SDI) regions and the fastest growth in prevalence cases occurring in Oceania, Qatar, Niger, and the United Arab Emirates. Age-specific patterns show peak prevalence in women aged 25-29 years and the highest mortality in women aged 45-49, likely attributable to physiological decline, weakened immune response, and comorbid conditions. The increasing endometriosis burden is associated with economic development, Westernized lifestyles, high-calorie diets, obesity, delayed diagnosis and treatment, and aging populations. Regional variations in disease burden, particularly in Oceania, Eastern Europe, and high-income Asia Pacific regions, suggest that lifestyle factors, environmental influences, and advances in diagnostic technologies significantly contribute to the observed epidemiological trends.

title: Anything but Endo: Diagnostic Buck‐Passing in Endometriosis Diagnosis
summary: This qualitative sociology study examines the diagnostic challenges that delay endometriosis diagnosis by analyzing how healthcare providers engage in "diagnostic buck-passing"—referring patients between specialists without reaching a definitive diagnosis. Endometriosis causes a constellation of debilitating symptoms including pelvic pain, heavy periods, back pain, nausea, and acute pain during menstruation, ovulation, bowel movements, and intercourse, with approximately one-third of patients developing infertility. The diagnostic confusion is compounded by symptom overlap with conditions like irritable bowel syndrome, interstitial cystitis, and autoimmune disorders, combined with significant knowledge gaps resulting from systematic underinvestment in women's health research and contested understanding of endometriosis etiology. Beyond biomedical factors, environmental pollutants, stress, and adverse childhood experiences have been associated with endometriosis development. The article argues that prolonged diagnostic journeys are perpetuated not merely by clinical uncertainty, but by stigma surrounding women's pain and its normalization in medical practice, with laparoscopic surgery remaining the only definitive diagnostic method. These findings highlight how systemic barriers in medical knowledge and practice create delays in diagnosis and treatment for endometriosis patients seeking care.

title: Endometriosis and eating disorders: epidemiology, shared neurobiology, and clinical implications
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis shows a significantly elevated association with eating disorders, with women affected by the condition having nearly threefold higher odds of developing disordered eating behaviors compared to the general population. The underlying pathophysiology involves dysregulation of multiple appetite-regulating systems including leptin, endocannabinoids, dopamine, and brain-derived neurotrophic factor, alongside the chronic systemic inflammation and pain characteristic of endometriosis. A bidirectional relationship exists whereby endometriosis-associated chronic pain, systemic inflammation, and hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis dysfunction directly contribute to disordered eating, while conversely, nutritional imbalances and metabolic disturbances from eating disorders can exacerbate endometriosis pathology. Additionally, ectopic lesions promote a proinflammatory microenvironment that drives pain sensitization and neuroangiogenesis, which together with infertility create profound body dissatisfaction—a critical risk factor for eating disorder development. Clinical management should incorporate Mediterranean diet recommendations and supervised exercise while recognizing that restrictive diets and excessive exercise may paradoxically promote obsessive eating behaviors. Comprehensive treatment requires integrated gynecological and psychiatric approaches addressing both the somatic manifestations of endometriosis and the psychological sequelae that increase vulnerability to eating pathology.

title: Experiences of women with endometriosis & their partners in India: Findings from a qualitative study
journal: The Indian Journal of Medical Research
summary: This qualitative study reveals endometriosis as a chronic inflammatory condition with profound multisystem effects on Indian women and their partners. The disease manifests through multiple pain syndromes including dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and bladder/bowel pain, with approximately 62% of the 21 enrolled women experiencing infertility. Beyond physical symptoms, endometriosis causes significant psychological distress—including anxiety, depression, helplessness, and in severe cases suicidal ideation—alongside substantial impacts on employment, social engagement, and financial stability due to high treatment costs. Partners experience secondary emotional and financial burden, with men reporting helplessness and anxiety while often assuming expanded caretaker responsibilities. Treatment approaches include laparoscopy surgery, pain medications, hormonal therapy, and self-directed coping strategies like exercise and dietary modifications, though delayed diagnosis substantially worsens outcomes. The study emphasizes that early diagnosis is crucial for timely intervention and symptom validation, and highlights a critical knowledge gap regarding endometriosis awareness among both patients and partners in India.

title: Endometriosis and expressions of self-management and resilience among Asian women living in Canada during the COVID-19 pandemic: A photovoice study
summary:
# Summary

This photovoice study examined endometriosis management and resilience among Asian women living in Canada during the COVID-19 pandemic. Endometriosis, which disproportionately affects Asian women—particularly those of Filipino, Japanese, Indian, and Korean descent—is characterized by chronic pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, lower back and abdominal pain, dyspareunia, and dyschezia, with infertility affecting up to half of cases and considerable psychological distress reducing quality of life. The COVID-19 pandemic disrupted endometriosis healthcare services while exacerbating physical and psychological symptoms including dysmenorrhea, pelvic pain, fatigue, anxiety, and depression, compounded by discrimination and reduced access to professional care. Asian women demonstrated resilience through multiple self-management strategies (heat, rest, meditation, exercise, diet, yoga, medication) and coping mechanisms including problem-focused coping, planning ahead, artistic expression, and journaling, which reduced pain and stress symptoms. Social support—from family, partners, friends, and pets—and emotional validation proved pivotal to coping, though cultural norms sometimes limited this support, whereas healthcare provider knowledge and empowering actions fostered inner strength and resilience during this challenging period.

title: Information-Seeking Behaviours of CALD Women with Endometriosis in Australia: A Qualitative Study
journal: International Journal of Environmental Research and Public Health
summary: Endometriosis, which affects one in seven Australian women, causes dysmenorrhoea, dyspareunia, chronic pain, and infertility, yet culturally and linguistically diverse (CALD) women face substantial barriers to diagnosis and care. Language barriers, communication difficulties, and low health literacy inhibit healthcare service access, while menstrual taboos, cultural and religious beliefs, and associated stigma actively discourage women from seeking information and disclosing symptoms to healthcare providers. CALD communities demonstrate particular vulnerability, with Black women experiencing lower diagnostic rates and CALD parents preferring summarised, visually-presented health information and trusting social media sources more than mainstream medical resources. Although women value tailored information from healthcare providers and practical insights from those with lived experience, many providers have limited endometriosis knowledge, leaving women to navigate abundant but often overwhelming online resources. These findings underscore the critical need for culturally-sensitive, accessible health information that addresses information-seeking behaviours specific to CALD populations to improve diagnostic outcomes and quality of life for this underserved population.

title: Developing a Trauma-Informed Social Media Campaign to Disseminate Endometriosis-Specific Qualitative Art-Based Research Findings: Tutorial
summary: Endometriosis affects approximately 10% of reproductive-aged women and causes debilitating pelvic pain alongside painful periods, painful sexual intercourse, and infertility, while patients frequently experience medical dismissal and emotional distress in healthcare encounters. East and Southeast Asian women face disproportionate disease severity, being eight times more likely than White counterparts to experience severe endometriosis. The COVID-19 pandemic exacerbated psychological symptoms including depression and anxiety in this population, prompting the EndoPhoto Study to document experiences of South, East, and Southeast Asian women during the pandemic. Trauma-informed social media campaigns have emerged as an evidence-based approach to disseminate endometriosis research findings while improving patient mental health outcomes and satisfaction, though traditional social media algorithms risk retraumatization and stigmatization. The study demonstrates that trauma-informed design principles are essential when using social media platforms for patient education, peer support, and advocacy in endometriosis care. This approach addresses both the clinical burden of endometriosis and the psychological harm caused by medical dismissal and social isolation.

title: Development and validation of a Chinese version of the EHP-30 for EMS patients: Assessing quality of life and informing targeted interventions
summary: Endometriosis is a chronic gynecological condition that causes severe pelvic pain, infertility, and significant psychological impacts including anxiety and depression, substantially impairing quality of life. This study developed and validated a Chinese version of the Endometriosis Health Profile-30 (EHP-30), a multidimensional assessment tool comprising four domains: physical function, mental and psychological function, social function, and disease characteristics and treatment. The revised scale demonstrated good reliability and validity with a Cronbach α coefficient of 0.780, and higher scale scores correlated with improved quality of life in patients. Additionally, the researchers constructed the first theoretical framework for eHealth literacy in endometriosis patients, demonstrating that enhanced digital health literacy is associated with better disease understanding, improved self-management, and informed decision-making. The integration of psychosocial parameters—including emotion management and self-perception—within this framework enables development of personalized, holistic care interventions. These validated tools provide clinicians with scientifically grounded instruments to comprehensively assess endometriosis impact and guide targeted therapeutic strategies that address the physical, psychological, and social dimensions of the disease.

title: Understanding Inflammatory Bowel Disease in Women From Puberty to Menopause
summary: Women with inflammatory bowel disease experience distinct clinical challenges across reproductive life stages due to interconnected inflammatory, hormonal, and gastrointestinal mechanisms. During puberty, chronic inflammation, corticosteroid exposure, and malnutrition impair pubertal development and disrupt hormonal regulation, while fluctuating estrogen and progesterone levels directly exacerbate both gastrointestinal symptoms and IBD activity. Notably, endometriosis and uterine fibroids frequently co-occur with IBD, contributing to pelvic pain and sexual dysfunction that complicate symptom management and clinical diagnosis. The systemic inflammatory state associated with IBD significantly reduces peak bone mass accrual during critical developmental periods and increases osteoporosis risk postmenopausally. As women transition to menopause, declining estrogen further impairs gastrointestinal motility and immune regulation, while sustained systemic inflammation and hormonal changes elevate cardiovascular risk in this population. These findings underscore the need for integrated gynecologic and gastroenterologic management tailored to women's hormonal life stages.

title: Risk of cardiovascular disease and mortality among women with endometriosis: A systematic review and meta‐analysis
summary:
# Summary

This systematic review and meta-analysis demonstrates that women with endometriosis face significantly elevated cardiovascular disease risk, with pooled analyses showing increased risks of stroke (1.18-fold), coronary heart disease (1.36-fold), myocardial infarction, and composite cardiovascular disease outcomes. The underlying mechanisms linking endometriosis to cardiovascular disease involve chronic inflammation and an atherogenic lipid profile, which collectively promote atherothrombotic disease development and endothelial dysfunction, leading to arterial stiffness and blood pressure elevation. Additional cardiovascular risk pathways in endometriosis include metabolic complications (metabolic syndrome, hypercholesterolemia, hypertriglyceridemia), hypertensive pregnancy disorders, and early menopause—the latter occurring naturally or iatrogenically through bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy. While gonadotropin-releasing hormone agonists effectively reduce endometriosis-related pain by suppressing ovarian function, this estrogen reduction paradoxically increases cardiovascular risk, highlighting a critical treatment trade-off. Surgical treatment of endometriosis appears to improve endothelial dysfunction, suggesting potential cardiovascular benefits from disease management, though comprehensive screening and preventive strategies for cardiovascular risk are warranted in this population.

title: Impacts of the COVID-19 pandemic on the mental health of Asian women with endometriosis in Canada: A photovoice study
summary: This photovoice study examined how the COVID-19 pandemic intensified mental health challenges in Asian women with endometriosis in Canada, revealing a complex interplay of biological and social factors. Endometriosis itself causes significant physical symptoms including painful periods, sexual dysfunction, infertility, and chronic pelvic pain, which are independently associated with high rates of depression and anxiety, affecting up to one-third of those diagnosed. The pandemic exacerbated these mental health burdens through multiple pathways: worsening endometriosis symptoms, disruption of healthcare services, social isolation, and reduced access to support resources. Additionally, Asian participants faced compounded psychological distress from pandemic-related anti-Asian racism and discrimination, which contributed to elevated anxiety, depression, fear, and panic compared to White counterparts. The findings highlight a bidirectional relationship where endometriosis-related pain, symptom invisibility, and fertility uncertainty directly trigger mental health challenges including isolation, hopelessness, and suicidal ideation, while poor mental health further impairs coping capacity. Collectively, these disruptions created a multidimensional crisis affecting participants' sense of control, identity, and overall wellbeing, underscoring the urgent need for integrated mental health support in endometriosis care, particularly for marginalized populations.

title: Impact of adverse childhood experiences on health-related quality of life in Australian women with endometriosis: a population-based cohort study
summary:
# Summary

This population-based cohort study reveals that Australian women with endometriosis who experienced adverse childhood experiences (ACEs) demonstrate significantly worse health-related quality of life across multiple domains. A clear dose-response relationship emerges, where cumulative ACE exposure correlates with poorer outcomes in pain severity, emotional well-being, social support, self-image, and sense of control. Specific types of childhood trauma—including psychological abuse, physical abuse, and sexual abuse—consistently associate with greater impairment, with sexual abuse showing particularly strong associations with social support needs (nearly doubled odds). The study demonstrates that trauma severity matters: women reporting very or extremely traumatic experiences show substantially worse outcomes across all quality of life domains compared to those without such trauma history. These findings suggest that screening for childhood trauma and providing integrated psychological support may be critical components of comprehensive endometriosis care to address the compounded burden on quality of life in affected women.

title: Online-CBT for endometriosis: Exploring the acceptability and impact of the Ed.iTh program
summary: Endometriosis causes a complex burden of physical symptoms including dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, chronic pelvic pain, and infertility, alongside significant psychological distress characterized by depression, anxiety, loss of control, and diminished self-esteem that collectively predict long-term functional impairment. The Ed.iTh program is an eight-module internet-based cognitive-behavioral therapy intervention that addresses these interconnected symptoms through psychoeducation, cognitive restructuring, pacing, emotion regulation, and biopsychosocial content grounded in CBT principles. The intervention targets maladaptive patterns common in endometriosis populations, such as avoidance and catastrophizing, while also addressing stress and pain management, communication, and goal maintenance. Participants found the program acceptable and appreciated its relevance, information quality, and emotional support, reporting reduced feelings of isolation through exposure to biopsychosocial content that validated their experiences. Meta-analytic evidence demonstrates that internet-based CBT produces outcomes comparable to face-to-face therapy for chronic pain, depression, anxiety, and fatigue, with sustained effects at follow-up. These findings establish iCBT as a promising low-threshold treatment option for endometriosis that can improve both pain symptoms and psychological outcomes while enhancing quality of life.

title: Epidemioclinical profile and psychological experience of women consulting for primary infertility at the university hospital of angre about 160 cases
summary: This epidemiological study of 160 women with primary infertility identified multiple interconnected biological and psychological factors contributing to reproductive failure. Tubal obstruction (36.5%), menstrual cycle irregularities, and uterine fibroids (29% combined) emerged as the predominant structural causes, while polycystic ovary syndrome and low anti-müllerian hormone levels indicating reduced ovarian reserve were significant endocrine contributors. Genital and upper reproductive tract infections, particularly with histories of salpingitis and sexually transmitted infections, posed substantially elevated risks mediated through pelvic inflammation and tubal damage. The psychological burden was profound, with anxiety (81.9%), stress (71.9%), and sexual disturbances (56.3%) documented in the majority of patients, alongside substantial impacts on professional functioning and quality of life including depression and identity crises. Obesity and overweight status (29.3% prevalence) compounded infertility through menstrual irregularities and ovulation disorders, though lifestyle interventions including weight loss and physical activity offered therapeutic benefit. The study underscores the multifactorial nature of primary infertility requiring integrated diagnostic approaches and comprehensive management addressing both medical pathology and psychological support.

title: Leveraging health records to identify diagnoses associated with recurrent pregnancy loss across two medical centers
summary:
# Summary

This multi-center study leveraged electronic health records from UCSF and Stanford to systematically identify diagnoses associated with recurrent pregnancy loss (RPL), validating known risk factors and uncovering novel associations. Established RPL risk factors including advanced maternal age, chromosomal abnormalities, congenital uterine anomalies, antiphospholipid antibody syndrome, and hereditary thrombophilias were confirmed across both centers. The study revealed strong associations between RPL and multiple reproductive and gynecological conditions—including endometriosis, polycystic ovaries, ovarian dysfunction, dysmenorrhea, and pelvic inflammatory disease—as well as with immunological and metabolic disorders such as diffuse connective tissue disease, thyroiditis, and metabolic syndrome. Notably, paternal factors including sperm DNA fragmentation emerged as additional contributors to pregnancy loss, while negative associations with successful pregnancy outcomes (normal delivery, preeclampsia, obstetrical trauma) provided internal validation. Mental health conditions and glucose metabolism showed discordant associations between centers, suggesting the need for further investigation into their relationship with RPL.

title: Pain, emotional distress, and fear of recurrence or progression in people with endometriosis: a network approach
summary: This network analysis study examines how psychological and cognitive factors influence pain and emotional distress in endometriosis patients, a chronic condition affecting approximately 1 in 9 individuals assigned female at birth. The research reveals that fear of disease recurrence or progression (FORP) is remarkably prevalent, with nearly 85% of participants reporting severe levels, and this fear is strongly associated with greater physical pain, existential concerns, poorer well-being, and increased anxiety and depression symptoms. While endometriosis-related pain itself shows weak correlation with disease severity, psychosocial factors—particularly existential concerns and unhelpful metacognitive beliefs—emerge as key drivers of both FORP and emotional distress, with existential concerns uniquely contributing independent variance to fear levels. Notably, interpretation bias showed minimal association with FORP, suggesting that catastrophic thinking patterns about disease progression may be less important than deeper existential worries in this population. These findings highlight that psychological interventions targeting existential concerns and maladaptive thought patterns about one's health may be critical for managing not only emotional distress but also pain severity and quality of life in endometriosis patients.

title: Association of body mass index on sexual function in women with endometriosis: A cross-sectional study
summary:
# Summary

This cross-sectional study examined how body mass index (BMI) influences sexual function in women with endometriosis, a condition that causes dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, chronic pelvic pain, and chronic inflammation. Women with endometriosis demonstrated significantly lower sexual function scores (FSFI) compared to healthy controls, with this impairment strongly associated with endometriosis symptom burden. The analysis revealed a complex, non-linear relationship between BMI and sexual function: both underweight status and obesity (BMI > 30 kg/m²) correlated with lower FSFI scores, though the association with high BMI was particularly pronounced in women reporting seven endometriosis symptoms representing more severe disease. BMI may exacerbate sexual dysfunction through multiple pathways including inflammatory processes, dyspareunia, hormonal imbalance, and altered pain perception, while endometriosis itself can trigger weight changes through chronic pain, reduced physical activity, and psychological distress. The findings suggest that BMI and endometriosis symptom burden act synergistically to compound sexual dysfunction risk, highlighting the importance of considering both metabolic status and disease severity when addressing sexual health in endometriosis patients.

title: Perceived impact of medicinal cannabis on pelvic pain and endometriosis related symptoms in Aotearoa New Zealand: an observational cohort study
summary: This observational cohort study from Aotearoa New Zealand examined medicinal cannabis as a potential treatment for endometriosis-related symptoms, particularly pelvic pain and associated complications. Endometriosis, characterized by growth of endometrial-like tissue outside the uterus, causes severe pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and fatigue while functioning as a systemic inflammatory condition that impairs fertility. Participants using medicinal cannabis experienced significant symptom reduction over 12 weeks, with overall pain scores decreasing from 5.46 to 3.77 and quality of life scores (measured by EHP-30) improving markedly from 68.77 to 37.40. The therapeutic mechanisms likely involve endocannabinoid system dysregulation in endometriosis (characterized by elevated anandamide and 2-arachidonoylglycerol alongside reduced CB1 receptor expression), which cannabis phytochemicals modulate through effects on angiogenesis, apoptosis, nerve innervation, and cell migration. Beyond pain reduction, CBD oil improved sleep quality and anxiety—secondary effects that substantially enhanced quality of life and enabled some participants to return to work and improve fertility prospects. While medicinal cannabis proved generally well-tolerated compared to conventional analgesics like tramadol and codeine, minor adverse effects including headaches and drowsiness were reported in a small proportion of users.

title: Deciphering the shared genetic architecture between female reproductive disorders and psychiatric disorders
summary: This study reveals substantial shared genetic architecture between female reproductive disorders and psychiatric conditions. Endometriosis, infertility, and polycystic ovary syndrome show significant genetic correlations with major depressive disorder, bipolar disorder, and ADHD, with Mendelian randomization analysis demonstrating that major depressive disorder causally increases risk for both endometriosis and infertility. The researchers identified specific shared genetic loci across these conditions, including rs633891 linking endometriosis, infertility, and depression, rs9689096 and rs6650130 shared between endometriosis and ADHD, and rs11031047 between endometriosis and bipolar disorder. Mechanistically, dysregulation of the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis, inflammatory responses, and sex hormone abnormalities appear to underlie the observed associations between reproductive and psychiatric disorders. The gene ARL14EP was implicated as a candidate for the endometriosis-bipolar disorder link. These findings suggest that endometriosis and related reproductive disorders share common biological pathways with psychiatric conditions and highlight the importance of integrated clinical assessment and management of comorbid mental health symptoms in women with reproductive disorders.

title: “You don’t treat your skinny patients like this”: a qualitative study of fertility care experiences among women with larger bodies and infertility
summary: This qualitative study reveals how weight stigma in fertility care creates systemic barriers that harm women with larger bodies seeking treatment for infertility. Weight-related discrimination by healthcare providers causes psychological and physical harm while contributing to delayed or avoided care seeking, and when women do access fertility services, providers often make assumptions based on weight that lead to abbreviated diagnostic evaluations and missed critical diagnoses including hypothyroidism, PCOS, and antiphospholipid syndrome. Weight stigmatization causes providers to attribute infertility solely to body weight, preventing thorough evaluation of underlying conditions such as endometriosis and diminished ovarian reserve, while inappropriate treatments like metformin are prescribed without proper diagnostic justification. The anticipation of biased treatment based on prior stigmatizing experiences leads women to further delay or avoid fertility care altogether, ultimately resulting in negative fertility outcomes and compounded psychological distress. The study demonstrates that empathetic, humanizing care that incorporates shared decision-making and acknowledges patients' lived experiences significantly improves patient satisfaction and outcomes. These findings underscore how intersecting weight bias and structural discrimination in reproductive healthcare undermine diagnostic accuracy and access to appropriate fertility treatment for women with larger bodies.

title: Prevalence and associated factors of clinically significant symptoms of depression and anxiety among women with endometriosis in Iran
summary: This Iranian cross-sectional study examined the prevalence and determinants of depression and anxiety among women with endometriosis, revealing clinically significant depression in 59.8% and anxiety in 54.5% of participants—substantially higher rates than the general population. Endometriosis causes persistent pelvic pain through ectopic endometrial-like tissue implanted on the ovaries, fallopian tubes, and pelvic peritoneum, which triggers inflammation, bleeding, and adhesions that chronically activate the stress response. Disease duration and pain intensity emerged as the primary modifiable factors associated with depressive symptoms, while education level and age were significant correlates of anxiety, with pain experience proving more influential on mental health outcomes than anatomical disease stage. The underlying mechanisms linking endometriosis to psychological distress involve chronic inflammation, glial activation, and hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis dysregulation, alongside the direct psychological impact of continuous pain on occupational, social, and interpersonal functioning. Chronic pelvic pain additionally contributes to sleep disruption and social withdrawal, compounding mood disturbances and reducing health-related quality of life. These findings underscore that endometriosis should be recognized not merely as a gynecological condition but as a systemic disease requiring integrated management addressing both physical pain and mental health comorbidities.

title: Digital patient decision aids for endometriosis management: a scoping review protocol
summary: Endometriosis is a chronic, oestrogen-dependent inflammatory condition that causes a spectrum of physical and psychological symptoms including pelvic pain, dysmenorrhoea, dyspareunia, fatigue, infertility, depression, and anxiety. This scoping review protocol examines the emerging role of digital patient decision aids in endometriosis management, recognizing that current single-provider models of care inadequately address the disease's multifaceted nature. Patient decision aids have demonstrated effectiveness in enhancing patient knowledge and reducing decisional conflict while improving risk perception accuracy. Digital technologies, including chatbots and large language models powered by generative artificial intelligence, show promise in supporting shared medical decision-making through improved interactivity, personalization, and real-time engagement. These AI-based tools offer potential to enhance patient health literacy and engagement by providing 24/7 access to guidance and educational content. The review aims to synthesize evidence on how such digital aids can better support endometriosis patients in navigating treatment decisions within a multidisciplinary care framework.

title: What is the Evidence on Lifestyle Interventions for the Symptom Management of Pelvic Pain in Women With Endometriosis or Adenomyosis? A Scoping Review
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis and adenomyosis are conditions that commonly co-exist and cause multiple debilitating symptoms including dysmenorrhea, chronic pelvic pain, dyspareunia, dyschezia, menorrhagia, fatigue, and infertility. While conventional medical treatments such as NSAIDs, hormonal therapies, and tranexamic acid provide symptom relief, this scoping review identifies substantial evidence supporting lifestyle-based interventions for pain management in these conditions. Dietary approaches including Mediterranean diet, low FODMAP diet, and gluten-free diet, along with physical activity programs (Physio-EndEA), mind-body interventions (yoga, mindfulness), and digital health tools (Endocare, School of Endo, Endo-App) demonstrate significant reductions in pelvic pain and related symptoms. Nutritional supplements including vitamins C and E, fish oil, and garlic show moderate evidence for reducing dysmenorrhea, while transcutaneous electrical nerve stimulation (TENS) effectively reduces multiple pain phenotypes. These lifestyle interventions work partly by reducing systemic inflammation and improving sleep quality, both of which directly impact pain response and inflammatory status in endometriosis and adenomyosis.

title: Women’s Reproductive Health Conditions and Fertility Goals
summary:
# Summary

This study examines how common reproductive health conditions affect women's fertility goals and intentions, revealing a striking paradox: women diagnosed with conditions such as endometriosis, uterine fibroids, or pelvic inflammatory disease are 34% more likely to desire children than their unaffected peers, yet 35% less likely to intend to have children once they express that desire. Endometriosis, affecting over one in ten women, exemplifies this challenge by causing significant pain, digestive problems, and conception difficulties while simultaneously increasing rates of depression, anxiety, and social relationship strain. Other prevalent conditions like uterine fibroids (affecting over 70% of women) cause heavy bleeding and sexual pain while extending time to conception and raising preterm birth risk. The findings demonstrate that reproductive health conditions create substantial barriers to fulfilling fertility goals, with impacts extending beyond physical symptoms to encompass mental health, work productivity, and healthcare costs, underscoring the need for comprehensive clinical and psychosocial support.

title: Information Needs of Women Affected by Endometriosis and Their Environment: Qualitative Results from Participatory Workshops
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is a chronic condition associated with multiple debilitating symptoms including chronic pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and gastrointestinal complications, alongside significant psychological sequelae such as depression and anxiety. This qualitative research highlights that endometriosis substantially reduces quality of life through pain-related functional impairment, infertility concerns, strained relationships, and socioeconomic consequences including loss of productivity and income. A major barrier to optimal care is low endometriosis literacy among both patients and healthcare providers, which contributes to substantial diagnostic delays and inadequate treatment discussions. The study demonstrates that evidence-based information plays a critical role in improving doctor-patient communication, disease self-management, and patient autonomy, while participatory research approaches that integrate patient expertise can reduce stigma and enhance the relevance of health resources. To address identified information gaps, the authors advocate for a centralized, co-created digital information ecosystem that provides comprehensive, modular, and life-stage-tailored content on disease progression and multimodal treatment options including pharmacological, surgical, dietary, and complementary approaches.

title: From pain to policy: Improving endometriosis awareness, diagnosis, and treatment
summary: Endometriosis is a chronic inflammatory condition causing severe menstrual pain, heavy bleeding, chronic pelvic pain, infertility, and gastrointestinal symptoms, with far-reaching systemic health consequences. Beyond reproductive dysfunction, women with endometriosis face substantially elevated risks of multiple comorbidities, including nearly threefold increased risk of irritable bowel syndrome, 23% higher cardiovascular disease risk, and more than twofold increased rates of depression, anxiety, and eating disorders, alongside associations with autoimmune diseases, genitourinary disorders, and neoplasms. Current disease management emphasizes symptom control through non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs like ibuprofen, hormonal contraceptive pills, and surgical removal of lesions and adhesions to limit long-term complications. Educational interventions targeting menstrual health awareness in adolescents have demonstrated measurable success in improving knowledge and enabling earlier recognition of endometriosis symptoms, suggesting that public health policy incorporating school-based education programs could substantially reduce diagnostic delays and improve patient outcomes.

title: DRIVE-KG: Enhancing variant-phenotype association discovery in understudied complex diseases using heterogeneous knowledge graphs
journal: Pacific Symposium on Biocomputing. Pacific Symposium on Biocomputing
summary:
# Summary

This study presents DRIVE-KG, a heterogeneous knowledge graph framework that integrates multi-omics data from eQTL, GWAS, and PheWAS studies to discover novel genetic variants associated with endometriosis and related conditions. Using node2vec embeddings and multilayer perceptron link prediction, the approach identified 66 high-confidence SNP-endometriosis associations, notably revealing enrichment in obesity-related variants (24.2%), lipid metabolism genes (6%), and depressive disorder loci (4.5%), with specific variants like rs1420092 linked to inflammatory mediators IL-1 and IL-8. The framework substantially improved heritability explanation for endometriosis from 17% to 24.6%, and a graph convolutional network classifier achieved superior performance (F1=0.752) compared to traditional genetic risk scores (F1=0.698) for patient-level endometriosis/adenomyosis classification. These findings underscore the importance of multi-omics integration in deciphering the complex genetic architecture of endometriosis, an understudied disease affecting approximately 10% of reproductive-age women, and reveal important comorbidity associations with metabolic and psychiatric conditions that may inform clinical management strategies.

title: Economic evaluation of a digital symptom checker for endometriosis using a Markov decision process model
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is a chronic gynecological condition affecting 6–10% of women of reproductive age, characterized by inflammation, pelvic pain, infertility, and significantly reduced quality of life. Diagnosis is typically delayed by seven or more years due to non-specific symptoms, limited disease awareness, and lack of non-invasive diagnostic tests, resulting in substantial healthcare costs and productivity losses estimated at billions annually in the United States. This economic evaluation demonstrates that Flo SC, a digital symptom checker tool, reduces diagnostic delay by approximately 4.36 years and generates cost savings of $5,196 per patient by enabling earlier symptom recognition and prompting timely clinical evaluation. The cost-effectiveness of the intervention depends critically on tool accuracy (sensitivity and specificity ≥0.7), user compliance (>45%), and sustained adoption over at least a 10-year time horizon, with higher compliance yielding greater cost savings while low compliance (30%) renders the intervention cost-ineffective. Digital symptom checkers deliver the greatest economic and health benefits for conditions like endometriosis that are characterized by prolonged diagnostic delays, stigma, and low care-seeking rates, with success dependent on both technical performance and sustained user adoption and adherence.

title: The impact of endometriosis on educational and professional pathways: a cross-sectional online study
summary:
# Summary

This cross-sectional study examined how endometriosis disrupts educational and professional pathways in women, revealing substantial impacts on life trajectories. Endometriosis causes multiple debilitating symptoms—dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, chronic pelvic pain, fatigue, and sleep disturbances—alongside psychological burden including anxiety, depression, and psychological distress, which collectively reduce quality of life and impair intimate relationships. The disease significantly disrupts education through frequent school absences (55% in severely affected groups), impaired academic performance (38%), and reduced exam preparation ability (34%), while work disruption is even more pronounced, with 75-80% experiencing reduced productivity, 48-52% reporting frequent work absences, and substantial rates of job loss (18-22%), workplace discrimination (24-29%), and forced part-time employment (22-26%). Adolescent symptom onset combined with diagnostic delay creates the greatest impact, with these factors showing independent and indirect effects on both educational and work disruption; earlier symptom onset and prolonged diagnostic delay (experienced by 92% of those with adolescent onset) each significantly exacerbate educational and occupational consequences. The study demonstrates that endometriosis extends beyond medical concerns to create broader social inequalities, with women reporting diminished life outlook and foregone career opportunities, particularly when symptoms emerge during formative educational years.

title: The effect of endometriosis on fertility: Results of the National Health and Nutrition Examination and Mendelian randomization analysis, 1999 to 2006
summary: This study examined the relationship between endometriosis and fertility outcomes using National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey data combined with Mendelian randomization analysis. Endometriosis was strongly associated with reduced fertility, with 30-50% of women with the condition experiencing infertility and women with endometriosis having significantly fewer live births (1.91 vs 2.26 children) and lower spontaneous pregnancy rates (2-10% vs normal population rates). Beyond infertility, endometriosis caused multiple clinical manifestations including chronic pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, abnormal menstruation, and increased risks of spontaneous miscarriage and ectopic pregnancy. The study also identified epidemiological associations, finding endometriosis more prevalent in non-Hispanic White women and those with higher education levels, as well as comorbidities including uterine fibroids and abnormal uterine bleeding. Mendelian randomization analysis using inverse variance weighted and weighted median methods confirmed that endometriosis significantly reduced the odds ratio for number of children born, with no evidence of horizontal pleiotropy affecting the results. These findings collectively demonstrate endometriosis as a major determinant of reduced reproductive capacity through multiple mechanisms affecting pregnancy establishment and maintenance.

title: Risk of postpartum depression among women with endometriosis: the Norwegian mother, father and child cohort study (MoBa)
summary:
# Summary

Women with endometriosis face a substantially elevated risk of postpartum depression (PPD), with an adjusted relative risk of 1.34 compared to women without the condition, as demonstrated in this large Norwegian cohort study. This association is primarily mediated by a higher lifetime prevalence of major depression among women with endometriosis, which explains a large proportion of the increased PPD risk. Notably, infertility paradoxically demonstrates a protective effect, reducing PPD risk in women with endometriosis who experience fertility difficulties, while women with endometriosis who conceived without fertility problems showed the highest PPD risk at 1.61 times the baseline rate. The elevated mental health burden in endometriosis is attributed to multiple mechanisms including chronic pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, and dyspareunia that impair quality of life, combined with inflammatory pathways involving macrophage recruitment, cytokine secretion, and estrogen dominance that may alter brain chemistry and stress responses. Additionally, postpartum hormonal changes, particularly estrogen withdrawal, may further increase PPD vulnerability in women with pre-existing endometriosis-related neurobiological alterations.

title: Bridging Clinical Care and Self-Management: Impact of Nursing Module Based on WHO’s Universal Self-Care Framework in Women with Endometriosis
summary: Endometriosis is a chronic, estrogen-dependent inflammatory disorder associated with multiple debilitating symptoms including pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, menorrhagia, infertility, fatigue, anxiety, and depression, which collectively compromise quality of life and emotional well-being. A nursing-led self-care intervention based on WHO's Universal Self-Care Framework and Orem's Self-Care Deficit Nursing Theory significantly improved self-care compliance and reduced symptom severity, with a strong negative correlation observed between self-care practices and pain at one month (r = −0.70, p < 0.001). The comprehensive intervention incorporated diverse self-care strategies including dietary modifications (anti-inflammatory diet, increased fruit and vegetable consumption, fatty fish, plant oils, and reduced red meat), stress management techniques (mindfulness, breathing exercises, progressive muscle relaxation, and acupressure), lifestyle practices (adequate sleep, hydration, light exercise, and medication compliance), and psychosocial support through peer interaction and counseling. By the third month, the intervention group demonstrated near-complete adherence to personal hygiene practices and significantly higher self-care scores compared to controls, with younger age correlated with greater engagement in self-care practices. These findings demonstrate that integrating nursing-led self-care education into routine endometriosis management enhances patient empowerment, health literacy, and overall quality of life while addressing both physical symptoms and psychological comorbidities.

title: Global Trends in Endometriosis Burden, 1990–2021: A Sociodemographic Analysis Using GBD 2021 Data and Projections to 2036
summary:
# Summary

This Global Burden of Disease 2021 analysis reveals endometriosis as a significant chronic inflammatory disorder affecting millions worldwide, strongly associated with debilitating symptoms including pelvic pain, infertility, anxiety, and depression. The disease burden is unequally distributed globally, with South Asia, East Asia, and Southeast Asia experiencing the highest age-standardized prevalence and incidence rates, while India and China account for the largest absolute numbers of cases. Age-specific patterns show incidence peaks in women aged 20–24 years with highest prevalence at 25–29 years, and notably high incidence in adolescents (15–19 years), identifying this group as critical for intervention. Risk factors including age, geographic location, ethnicity, genetic predisposition, and body mass index contribute to disease susceptibility. Absolute health inequalities in endometriosis metrics decreased from 1990 to 2021, yet relative inequalities increased favoring wealthier populations, with population growth accounting for approximately 389.8% of the increased burden and aging contributing 12.5% to prevalence increases.

title: Endometriosis beyond the pelvis: Insights from atypical presentations leading to diagnostic delays
summary: Endometriosis presents with diverse clinical manifestations extending far beyond the pelvis, often causing diagnostic delays due to atypical presentations. Classic pelvic disease causes dysmenorrhoea, chronic pelvic pain, dyspareunia, and infertility through well-characterized mechanisms, but extragonadal involvement can lead to ascites, thoracic complications including catamenial pneumothorax and pleural effusions, gastrointestinal obstruction and bleeding, and urological dysfunction with hydronephrosis. The pathophysiology involves chronic peritoneal irritation, cyclical bleeding from ectopic implants, altered lymphatic dynamics, and transdiaphragmatic migration of endometrial tissue through anatomical fenestrations. Beyond organ-specific sequelae, endometriosis associates with significant systemic comorbidities including postural orthostatic tachycardia syndrome, autonomic dysfunction, chronic fatigue, anxiety, and depression, with chronic pain amplifying psychological symptoms. Notably, endometriosis carries malignant potential through chronic inflammation, oxidative stress, and repeated tissue injury contributing to transformation into clear cell and endometrioid ovarian cancers. Recognition of these atypical presentations is essential to prevent misdiagnosis as malignancy and enable timely intervention before irreversible organ damage occurs.

title: Using artificial intelligence to predict sexual health outcomes in endometriosis: a decision tree model algorithm
summary: Endometriosis causes significant sexual dysfunction through a multifactorial mechanism involving pain (dysmenorrhea and dyspareunia), hormonal disturbances, psychological distress, and inflammatory processes that progressively sensitize central pain pathways. Researchers developed a decision tree algorithm to identify predictors of sexual dysfunction in women with endometriosis, revealing that severe menstrual cramps, pain during urination or bowel movements, progressive worsening pain over time, obesity, low education, and not being in a couple all strongly predict sexual dysfunction with up to 100% probability in certain combinations. Conversely, the absence of dyspareunia, urinary or bowel pain, higher education level, and being in a committed relationship were protective factors associated with preserved sexual function. Beyond direct pain symptoms, endometriosis impacts quality of life, mental health, and intimate partnerships through complex interactions between emotional distress, relationship dynamics, infertility, and systemic effects. The artificial intelligence-based decision tree model successfully captured these multidimensional factors more effectively than traditional statistical approaches, providing a clinically useful tool for predicting sexual health outcomes and identifying high-risk women requiring targeted interventions.

title: Factors Associated with Infertility Among Women Attending Reproductive Health Centers in Mogadishu, Somalia: A Cross-Sectional Study
summary: This cross-sectional study of women in Mogadishu identified multiple reproductive and structural factors strongly associated with infertility. Fallopian tube blockage, polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS), and female genital mutilation emerged as the most significant risk factors, with adjusted odds ratios ranging from 10.5 to 20.4. Traditional harmful practices, particularly multiple cervix burns, demonstrated an exceptionally high association with infertility (AOR = 20.4). Notably, prior surgical interventions including myomectomy, tubal surgery, and cystectomy were protective against infertility, suggesting successful treatment of correctable gynecological conditions. Secondary infertility predominated in the region, linked to preventable causes including untreated sexually transmitted infections and pelvic inflammatory disease (reported in 66% of participants). Beyond fertility, the study highlighted significant comorbidities and psychosocial impacts, including associations between endometriosis and ovarian cancer, cardiovascular illness, and atopic disorders, as well as documented links between infertility and marital problems, intimate partner aggression, and financial strain.

title: Cross-platform comparison of the quality, reliability, and engagement of endometriosis-related videos on TikTok and Bilibili: A cross-sectional study
summary: Endometriosis, a chronic inflammatory condition affecting approximately 10% of women of reproductive age, causes pelvic pain, infertility, and reduced quality of life, though its nonspecific symptom presentation complicates diagnosis and management. This cross-sectional analysis compared endometriosis-related videos on TikTok and Bilibili, finding that while both platforms host predominantly professional content (83.6% of uploads), they differ significantly in structure and quality metrics: Bilibili videos are longer-form content with superior Global Quality Scores, modified DISCERN reliability scores, and JAMA benchmark adherence, reflecting the platform's emphasis on comprehensive explanations, whereas TikTok's short-video format drives substantially higher engagement through likes, comments, shares, and collections. Disease knowledge dominated content on Bilibili (81.2%), while treatment content was more prevalent on TikTok (42.4%) and notably generated the highest audience engagement despite shorter duration. Professional sources consistently achieved higher quality scores across all reliability measures compared to nonprofessional content, though engagement metrics showed weak correlation with quality indicators, suggesting a disconnect between what audiences interact with and what constitutes reliable medical information. These findings highlight a fundamental tension in social media dissemination of endometriosis information: platforms optimizing for viral engagement prioritize accessibility and brevity over informational depth and clinical reliability.

title: Endometriosis healthcare access in Indonesia based on the Indonesian national health insurance data in 2020–2023
summary: Endometriosis affects approximately 18% of women globally and is significantly associated with chronic pelvic pain, infertility, depression, and anxiety, substantially diminishing quality of life and imposing considerable economic burden through treatment costs and productivity loss. This analysis of Indonesian national health insurance data reveals that access to endometriosis care is severely constrained by socioeconomic status and geographic location, with patients in the lowest service classes and those outside Java and Bali regions receiving predominantly diagnostic-only care relying on ultrasound rather than surgical intervention. The maldistribution of healthcare infrastructure, including specialist physicians and laparoscopy facilities concentrated in Java, Bali, and Sumatra, creates persistent disparities in care access. Diagnostic delays compound disease progression and are driven by patient-related factors (symptom normalization), provider-related factors (misdiagnosis and dismissal), and health system barriers (specialist scarcity). Ultrasound-based diagnosis effectively detects endometriomas and deep-infiltrating endometriosis but fails to identify superficial peritoneal lesions, leading to underestimation of true disease prevalence. Addressing Indonesia's endometriosis burden requires equitable healthcare infrastructure investment, improved diagnostic accuracy with non-invasive imaging, and health system strengthening to reduce diagnostic delays and expand treatment access across socioeconomic and geographic groups.

title: Clinical Characterization of Genital Tract Malformations: A Retrospective Cohort Study on Associations with Endometriosis and Pregnancy Outcomes
summary:
# Summary

This retrospective cohort study reveals that obstructive genital tract malformations significantly increase endometriosis risk, particularly early-onset and severe disease, with retrograde menstruation and KRAS-mutated endometrial epithelial cells serving as underlying mechanisms. Hematometra retention in endometrium-containing residual horn subtypes (IIa/b) further facilitates endometriosis progression. Vaginal septa and residual uterine horns confer the highest infertility risks, with hysteroscopic metroplasty dramatically improving fertility outcomes (84.6% live birth rate versus 3.7% without intervention). Adolescent-onset endometriosis serves as a clinical warning sign for occult obstructive anomalies, while non-obstructive malformations frequently remain undiagnosed until fertility evaluation despite harboring stage I endometriosis lesions. Molecular abnormalities in HOXA10 and VEGF receptor genes in septate uteri may contribute to infertility, and MRI imaging proves essential for detecting both complex anatomical anomalies and associated endometriosis phenotypes, including ovarian endometriomas and deep infiltrating disease.

title: Information Needs of Culturally and Linguistically Diverse Women with Endometriosis in Australia: A Qualitative Study
journal: International Journal of Environmental Research and Public Health
summary: Endometriosis is a prevalent chronic condition affecting approximately one in seven women of reproductive age in Australia, characterized by abnormal growth of endometrial-like tissue outside the uterus, typically within the pelvic cavity. The condition manifests through multiple symptom clusters including dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, chronic pain, and bowel and bladder dysfunction that significantly impact quality of life. Current first-line treatments include hormonal contraception and pain medication to suppress symptoms, with surgical excision of endometriotic lesions employed when clinically indicated. This qualitative study focused on culturally and linguistically diverse (CALD) women with endometriosis in Australia, highlighting an underserved population whose unique healthcare needs warrant attention. The research underscores the critical role of health literacy in endometriosis management, as lower health literacy in CALD populations is associated with reduced engagement in preventative healthcare and poorer overall health outcomes, suggesting that tailored health education and culturally sensitive interventions are essential for improving care and outcomes in this vulnerable population.

title: Understanding the diagnostic process of endometriosis in primary care: An interview study with general practitioners in Denmark using clinical vignettes
journal: The European Journal of General Practice
summary: Endometriosis presents a complex diagnostic challenge in primary care due to its multifaceted clinical presentation and substantial burden on patients. The condition causes multiple interconnected symptoms including pelvic pain, heavy menstrual bleeding, dyspareunia, dysuria, dyschezia, fatigue, and subfertility, while also triggering chronic pain, stress, anxiety, and depression that significantly impair quality of life and work capacity. A critical diagnostic barrier is the 7-9 year delay commonly experienced, which may worsen disease progression and fertility outcomes; general practitioners more readily recognize endometriosis when symptoms follow a cyclical menstrual pattern, but frequently misattribute gastrointestinal presentations such as abdominal pain, bloating, and bowel changes to irritable bowel syndrome instead. The overlapping symptom profiles between endometriosis and gastrointestinal conditions, combined with non-specific presentations and diagnostic hierarchies that prioritize ruling out malignancy, create substantial challenges for accurate identification. Inquiring systematically about the cyclical nature of symptoms may improve primary care diagnostic recognition and reduce the prolonged diagnostic delays that currently characterize endometriosis management.

title: Diagnostic value of MiRNAs in endometriosis: a systematic review and meta-analysis
summary:
# Summary

MicroRNAs (miRNAs) are dysregulated in endometriosis and show promise as non-invasive diagnostic biomarkers, with this systematic review identifying 39 upregulated and 72 downregulated miRNAs in affected individuals. MiRNAs function as gene expression regulators involved in key biological processes, and their dysregulation contributes to endometriosis pathogenesis through mechanisms including progesterone resistance and potentially malignant transformation. Endometriosis causes significant clinical morbidity including pelvic pain and infertility, which current diagnostic approaches using transvaginal ultrasound, magnetic resonance imaging, CA125, and invasive laparoscopy address suboptimally. Multiple miRNAs, notably hsa-miR-375-3p, hsa-miR-451-5p, and hsa-miR-320a, demonstrated robust diagnostic utility with area under the curve values ranging from 0.81 to 0.91 across various sample types including peripheral blood, endometrial tissue, and saliva. This meta-analysis establishes that miRNA profiling represents a viable alternative to invasive diagnostic methods, potentially enabling earlier screening and improved patient outcomes.

title: Improving access to fertility treatment in Asia Pacific: consensus from an expert forum
summary:
# Summary

This consensus article from an expert forum identifies reproductive disorders—particularly endometriosis, uterine fibroids, and polycystic ovarian syndrome—as major drivers of infertility in the Asia Pacific region, with advancing maternal age further compromising fertility outcomes. Beyond biological impacts, infertility imposes significant psychological and socioeconomic burdens, as studies from Taiwan and Australia document high rates of anxiety, depression, and psychological distress among women seeking treatment, while simultaneously reducing workforce participation and creating workplace harassment risks. Assisted reproductive technology (ART) represents a critical intervention for establishing pregnancy; however, its utilization remains heavily dependent on financial accessibility and the availability of public reimbursement programs, which vary considerably across Asia Pacific economies. The article emphasizes that declining fertility rates associated with untreated infertility contribute to broader population-level consequences including population aging and decline, creating urgent socioeconomic pressures. The expert consensus underscores that improving access to fertility treatment across the Asia Pacific region requires addressing not only the biological mechanisms of infertility but also the financial, psychological, and workplace-related barriers that currently limit treatment availability and effectiveness.

title: In utero and early life exposures in relation to endometriosis in adolescents and young adults
journal: European journal of obstetrics, gynecology, and reproductive biology
summary: This study examines how early life exposures influence endometriosis risk in adolescents and young adults, identifying protective and harmful environmental factors. Breastfeeding emerged as a significant protective factor, reducing endometriosis odds by 61% (OR=0.39), likely through enhanced immune function via immunologic proteins like IgA present in breastmilk.
