Conversely, maternal smoking during childhood substantially increased endometriosis risk through secondhand smoke exposure, potentially by disrupting immune and hormonal development during critical developmental windows. The study confirms established risk factors including earlier age at menarche and maternal history of endometriosis, while demonstrating that in utero and early life exposures can reprogram fetal development in ways that alter susceptibility to this disease characterized by severe pelvic pain and infertility. The majority of diagnosed cases presented as superficial peritoneal endometriosis, suggesting that immune dysfunction resulting from suboptimal early life conditions may facilitate the establishment of ectopic endometrial tissue. These findings highlight the importance of in utero and postnatal environmental factors in endometriosis pathogenesis.

title: Incident endometriosis diagnosis and anti-müllerian hormone (AMH): how surgical staging and typology relate to serum AMH levels
journal: American journal of obstetrics and gynecology
summary: This study demonstrates that endometriosis, a condition characterized by pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, and infertility, is associated with significantly reduced serum anti-müllerian hormone (AMH) levels, a key biomarker of ovarian reserve. The severity of AMH reduction correlates with endometriosis stage and type, with ovarian endometriomas showing the most pronounced effect (54.4% lower AMH levels), followed by stage III-IV disease (47.8% lower), and deep endometriosis (34.3% lower). The proposed mechanisms underlying these findings include increased inflammation and anatomic distortions caused by endometriosis lesions, which directly impair ovarian follicle quantity and function. Additionally, AMH and its receptor (AMHRII) are aberrantly expressed in both endometrial tissue and endometriosis lesions themselves, suggesting AMH may participate in endometriosis pathology beyond its role as a fertility marker. These findings indicate that endometriosis lesions independently compromise ovarian reserve through multiple pathways, potentially explaining the association between endometriosis and early natural menopause, and highlighting AMH's clinical utility in stratifying reproductive risk in affected women.

title: Study on material basis and network mechanism of the Guizhi Fuling pills in the treatment of endometriosis and endometrial polyps
summary:
# Summary

Guizhi Fuling pills, a traditional Chinese herbal formulation containing cinnamon twig, poria cocos, peach kernel, red peony, and moutan peonies, effectively treats both endometriosis and endometrial polyps, which commonly co-occur and share overlapping pathogenic mechanisms. Both conditions cause significant clinical morbidity including dysmenorrhea, infertility, abnormal uterine bleeding, and recurrent abortion through alterations in estrogen signaling, inflammatory responses, and immune dysregulation. The therapeutic mechanism of Guizhi Fuling pills involves key active compounds—β-sitosterol, luteolin, and quercetin—that modulate target proteins including the glucocorticoid receptor (NR3C1) and matrix metalloproteinase-9 (MMP9), ultimately engaging multiple signaling pathways central to disease pathogenesis. Treatment activates apoptosis while suppressing TNF, IL-17, toll-like receptor, VEGF, and NF-κB signaling cascades, alongside modulation of estrogen-dependent pathways. Gene ontology analysis further reveals that therapeutic effects involve regulation of circadian rhythm processes, mechanical stimulation responses, and DNA-binding regulation, suggesting the herbal formula addresses both systemic and local endometrial dysfunction underlying these gynecological conditions.

title: Patient satisfaction regarding medical care for endometriosis in Germany: an exploratory cross-sectional study
summary: Endometriosis causes multiple debilitating symptoms including dysmenorrhea, infertility, and pain during sexual intercourse, urination, and defecation, while imposing substantial physical, psychological, and social burdens that reduce quality of life. Current management approaches including hormonal therapies, analgesics, and surgical interventions can alleviate symptoms and improve quality of life, though no curative long-term therapy currently exists. This German cross-sectional study found that patient satisfaction with endometriosis care is significantly associated with fewer symptomatic days per month, reduced functional limitations, shorter diagnostic timelines, and confirmed diagnosis. Private health insurance and fewer initial symptoms were also associated with higher patient satisfaction. Since patient satisfaction serves as a key indicator of healthcare quality linked to improved health outcomes and reduced psychological burden, these findings suggest that expedited diagnostic pathways and effective symptom management are critical for enhancing the care experience of endometriosis patients.

title: Unraveling the link between early sexual initiation and endometriosis: evidence from population-based analyses and genetic causal inference
summary:
# Summary

This population-based and genetic causal inference study reveals a striking association between early age at first sexual intercourse and increased risk of endometriosis and adenomyosis, with delayed sexual initiation reducing endometriosis risk by approximately 30% and adenomyosis risk by 48%. The underlying mechanisms appear multifactorial: early sexual activity exposes individuals to elevated estrogen and progesterone concentrations, hormonal fluctuations, infections, and physical trauma, while psychological stress may dysregulate the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis. Endometriosis, characterized by estrogen-dependent growth of ectopic endometrial tissue and altered estrogen receptor expression, manifests clinically with severe pelvic pain, fatigue, bloating, and nausea, frequently progressing to depression, anxiety, and infertility. The related adenomyosis condition leads to uterine enlargement, abnormal bleeding, and dysmenorrhea, with growing evidence suggesting these diseases share overlapping pathological features. These findings suggest that reproductive timing may represent a modifiable risk factor for endometriosis and related disorders.

title: “Lead like a woman”: strengthening healthcare, medical imaging, and oncology through female leadership
summary: This article examines endometriosis within the context of female leadership in healthcare and medical imaging. The research highlights that endometriosis significantly impacts women's overall health and wellbeing, underscoring the need for comprehensive understanding of the disease's multifaceted effects on patient care. Additionally, endometriosis contributes to increased caregiving responsibilities among affected women, creating additional burdens that intersect with their professional and personal lives. The article suggests that strengthening female leadership in healthcare and medical imaging can address systemic barriers such as inadequate facility design and lack of workplace accommodations for those managing endometriosis-related caregiving obligations. By recognizing these challenges through a lens of female leadership, healthcare institutions can better support both patients with endometriosis and female healthcare professionals navigating these conditions. This perspective emphasizes that improving accessibility and workplace adjustments is essential for advancing women's health outcomes and professional equity in medical fields.

title: Development and Initial Psychometric Testing of a Patient-Reported Clinical Tool for Endometriosis: The Mobility Measure for Endometriosis (MobEndo)
summary:
# Summary

This study developed and validated the Mobility Measure for Endometriosis (MobEndo), a patient-reported tool designed to assess functional limitations caused by endometriosis. Endometriosis causes significant mobility impairments through multiple mechanisms, including pelvic chronic pain, dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and fatigue, which collectively result in functional disability and reduced quality of life. The MobEndo questionnaire comprises 18 items organized into two components: one measuring positional transitions and pelvic stability movements (bending, rising, stair climbing), and another assessing trunk extension and load-bearing tasks. Psychometric testing demonstrated excellent reliability with a Cronbach's alpha of 0.977 and test-retest ICC of 0.976, as well as strong concurrent and discriminant validity when correlated with existing pain and physical functioning scales. Factor analysis explained 71.78% of total variance with strong factor loadings (0.84–0.95), and known-groups analyses confirmed the tool effectively discriminates between patients with different pain severity levels. MobEndo represents a valid and reliable clinical instrument that addresses a gap in existing endometriosis assessment tools by comprehensively measuring physical functioning limitations beyond pain intensity alone.

title: Correlation Between Pain Intensity in Different Locations and Intraoperative Stage of Endometriosis According to rASRM and #ENZIAN Classification
summary: Endometriosis is a chronic inflammatory disease characterized by endometrium-like tissue within the abdominal cavity, affecting up to 80% of patients with chronic pain and up to 70% with persistent pelvic pain, while contributing to infertility in approximately one-third of cases and psychological distress including anxiety and depression. The disease manifests with heterogeneous symptoms including cyclic and chronic pelvic pain, dyspareunia, dyschezia, dysuria, abnormal uterine bleeding, and fatigue, with significant occupational consequences such as absenteeism and reduced productivity. This study demonstrates that the anatomical localization of lesions using the #ENZIAN classification system correlates more effectively with specific pain phenotypes than the traditional rASRM staging system, particularly showing that pain during micturition is associated with specific #ENZIAN classifications (Tm, T1, B1), while dyspareunia correlates with #ENZIAN P classification. Notably, lesion extent in the ovarian region (#ENZIAN O0) was associated with increased pain symptom frequency and multiple pain locations, whereas more extensive involvement (O3) showed reduced pain reporting. Management encompasses multiple conservative approaches including nutritional interventions, psychological support, analgesic therapy, hormonal treatment, and physiotherapy, alongside surgical options, with treatment selection informed by both disease stage and patient symptom profiles.

title: Does any serum marker predict the ovarian endometrioma accompanied with or without deep infiltrative endometriosis?
journal: Journal of the Chinese Medical Association : JCMA
summary: This study investigates serum biomarkers for predicting ovarian endometriomas with or without deep infiltrative endometriosis (DIE), a severe form of the chronic inflammatory disease affecting over 10% of women of reproductive age. Endometriosis causes multiple clinical complications including pelvic adhesions, chronic pelvic pain, and infertility, while DIE specifically associates with severe complications such as ureteral stricture. The researchers found that women with DIE exhibited significantly elevated serum levels of C-reactive protein (CRP) and carbohydrate antigen 125 (CA 125), as well as lower hemoglobin levels compared to those without DIE. Both CRP and CA 125 correlated with DIE severity based on nodularity extent. Notably, serum CA 125 emerged as an independent predictor for identifying women with ovarian endometriomas accompanied by DIE, though CRP's nonspecific inflammatory role limits its diagnostic specificity. These findings suggest that CA 125 may serve as a valuable preoperative diagnostic marker for distinguishing endometriomas complicated by DIE from uncomplicated cases.
